words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Paul, a bondservant of Christ Jesus, a called apostle, having been set apart for the gospel of God,
verses.note: GREEK: "Doulos" (δοῦλος, Strong's G1401) means "bondservant" or "slave," indicating total ownership by a master — Paul identifies himself as Christ's property before claiming any apostolic authority. "Aphōrismenos" (ἀφωρισμένος, G873) means "having been set apart," a perfect passive participle suggesting divine initiative; notably the same root as "Pharisee" (pharisaios), suggesting Paul was once set apart for Torah but is now set apart for the gospel. THEOLOGICAL: Paul's triple self-identification — bondservant, called apostle, set apart — establishes both his humility and his divine commission.

verses.flow: which He promised beforehand through His prophets in the holy Scriptures,

words.m: verb aorist middle participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: concerning His Son, the One having come from the seed of David according to the flesh,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "From the seed of David according to the flesh" directly fulfills the Davidic covenant of 2 Samuel 7:12-14, where God promised David an eternal heir. Paul establishes Jesus' legal and biological claim to the throne of Israel. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "kata sarka" (κατὰ σάρκα) — "according to the flesh" — is deliberately paired with "kata pneuma" in v. 4, creating a two-stage Christological confession: Davidic lineage in the flesh, divine Sonship in power.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: the One having been declared Son of God in power according to the Spirit of holiness by the resurrection from the dead — Jesus Christ our Lord —
verses.note: GREEK: "Horisthentos" (ὁρισθέντος, G3724) means "having been declared/appointed/marked out" — not "made" Son of God, but publicly designated as such. This is not adoptionism but a declaration of what was already true. "Hagiōsynēs" (ἁγιωσύνης, G42) is a rare word for holiness, occurring only three times in the NT. THEOLOGICAL: The resurrection is the decisive event that publicly declares Jesus' identity as the Son of God in power. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The title "Son of God" carries both Davidic royal overtones (Ps 2:7) and divine council implications — the unique Son enthroned above all other heavenly beings.

verses.flow: through whom we received grace and apostleship unto the obedience of faith among all the nations, for the sake of His name,
verses.note: GREEK: "Hypakoēn pisteōs" (ὑπακοὴν πίστεως) — "the obedience of faith" — is a genitive construction that likely functions as both subjective and objective genitive: obedience that springs from faith and obedience that consists in faith. This phrase bookends the entire letter (cf. Rom 16:26). THEOLOGICAL: Paul's apostleship is directed "among all the nations" (en pasin tois ethnesin), establishing from the outset the universal scope of the gospel beyond ethnic Israel.

verses.flow: among whom you also are, called ones of Jesus Christ —

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: to all those being in Rome, beloved of God, called saints: Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
First indeed, I give thanks to my God through Jesus Christ concerning all of you, because your faith is being proclaimed in the whole world.
For God is my witness, whom I serve in my spirit in the gospel of His Son, how unceasingly I make mention of you,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: always upon my prayers, beseeching if somehow now at last I may be prospered by the will of God to come to you.
For I long to see you, that I may impart to you some spiritual gift, unto the establishing of you,
and this is, to be mutually encouraged among you through the faith in one another, both yours and mine.

And I do not wish you to be ignorant, brothers, that many times I purposed to come to you — and was hindered until the present — that I might have some fruit also among you, just as also among the rest of the nations.
Both to Greeks and to barbarians, both to the wise and to the foolish, I am a debtor.
So, as for what is in me, I am eager to proclaim the gospel also to you who are in Rome.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: For I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God unto salvation to everyone who believes, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is widely recognized as the thesis statement of the entire epistle. "Dynamis theou" (δύναμις θεοῦ) — "power of God" — indicates the gospel is not merely information but God's active, effective force for rescue. STRUCTURE: The sequence "to the Jew first and also to the Greek" establishes the salvation-historical priority of Israel while affirming universal scope — a tension Paul will develop throughout chapters 9-11.

verses.flow: For the righteousness of God is being revealed in it from faith unto faith, just as it has been written: "But the righteous one shall live by faith."
verses.note: GREEK: "Ek pisteōs eis pistin" (ἐκ πίστεως εἰς πίστιν) — "from faith unto faith" — is a debated phrase; it likely means "from beginning to end by faith" or "from the faithfulness of God unto human faith." TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Habakkuk 2:4 (LXX), where the prophet awaited God's vindication of the righteous; Paul applies this to the present revelation of God's righteousness in Christ. THEOLOGICAL: "Dikaiosynē theou" (δικαιοσύνη θεοῦ) — "the righteousness of God" — is the central theological concept of Romans, denoting both God's own righteous character and the righteous status He grants to believers.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: For the wrath of God is being revealed from heaven upon all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men, who suppress the truth in unrighteousness,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The wrath of God is not capricious anger but His settled, holy opposition to all that violates His character. The present tense "apokalyptetai" (is being revealed) parallels the same verb in v. 17, creating a deliberate contrast: God's righteousness is being revealed in the gospel, and simultaneously His wrath is being revealed against suppression of truth. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This begins Paul's indictment of humanity, echoing the divine judgment pattern seen in Genesis 6 and Psalm 82, where God judges both human and spiritual rebellion.

verses.flow: because that which is known of God is manifest among them, for God has made it manifest to them.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: For His invisible things from the creation of the world, being understood by the things made, are clearly seen — both His eternal power and divine nature — so that they are without excuse.
verses.note: GREEK: "Theiotes" (θειότης, G2305) means "divine nature" or "divinity" — a hapax legomenon in the NT, distinct from "theotēs" (Col 2:9) which denotes the fullness of deity. "Theiotes" refers to the attributes of divinity visible in creation. "Anapologētous" (ἀναπολογήτους, G379) means "without a legal defense" — a forensic term indicating humanity stands in a cosmic courtroom with no valid excuse.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Because, having known God, they did not glorify Him as God or give thanks, but became futile in their reasonings, and their senseless heart was darkened.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Professing to be wise, they became fools,
and exchanged the glory of the incorruptible God for a likeness of an image of corruptible man, and of birds, and of four-footed creatures, and of creeping things.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse deliberately echoes the golden calf incident (Ex 32) and Psalm 106:20 LXX: "They exchanged their glory for the likeness of an ox." Paul universalizes Israel's paradigmatic sin as the pattern of all human idolatry. The descent from human form to birds to four-footed creatures to creeping things mirrors the order of creation in Genesis 1, suggesting idolatry is a systematic reversal of the created order. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The worship of images corresponding to created beings recalls Deuteronomy 4:19 and 32:8-9, where God allotted the nations to lesser spiritual beings after Babel, while retaining Israel for Himself.

verses.flow: Therefore God gave them over in the desires of their hearts unto uncleanness, for the dishonoring of their bodies among themselves,

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: who exchanged the truth of God for the lie, and worshipped and served the creature rather than the Creator, who is blessed unto the ages. Amen.
verses.note: GREEK: "To pseudei" (τῷ ψεύδει) — "the lie" — uses the definite article, suggesting not merely falsehood in general but THE lie, the primal deception that the creature can be worshipped in place of the Creator. THEOLOGICAL: "Worshipped and served" (esebásthēsan kai elátreusan) pairs reverential awe with cultic service, indicating full religious devotion misdirected toward creation. Paul interrupts his indictment with a doxology — "who is blessed unto the ages, amen" — reflexively honoring the Creator even while describing His dishonoring.

verses.flow: Because of this, God gave them over unto dishonorable passions. For even their females exchanged the natural use for that which is contrary to nature,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and likewise also the males, having abandoned the natural use of the female, were inflamed in their lust toward one another — males with males working out the shamelessness — and receiving in themselves the due recompense of their error.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: And just as they did not approve to hold God in knowledge, God gave them over unto a debased mind, to do the things not befitting,
verses.note: GREEK: There is a deliberate wordplay in the Greek: "ouk edokimasan" (they did not approve/test) results in God giving them over to an "adokimon noun" (a debased/unapproved mind). The root "dokimazō" (G1381) means to test or assay metals; failing the test, the mind becomes "adokimos" — rejected, counterfeit, worthless. THEOLOGICAL: This is the third "God gave them over" (paredōken, vv. 24, 26, 28), forming a terrible triad of divine judicial abandonment: first to bodily uncleanness, then to dishonorable passions, finally to a debased mind — a progressive surrender to the consequences of rejecting God.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: having been filled with all unrighteousness, wickedness, covetousness, malice; full of envy, murder, strife, deceit, maliciousness; whisperers,
slanderers, haters of God, insolent, arrogant, boasters, inventors of evil things, disobedient to parents,
senseless, covenant-breakers, without natural affection, unmerciful;

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: who, having fully known the righteous decree of God — that those who practice such things are worthy of death — not only do them, but also approve of those who practice them.
verses.note: GREEK: "Epignontes" (ἐπιγνόντες) is an aorist participle of "epiginōskō" (G1921), meaning "having fully known" — a stronger form than simple "ginōskō," indicating thorough, experiential knowledge. "Syneudokousin" (G4909) means to take pleasure together with, to co-approve — indicating active endorsement. THEOLOGICAL: Paul's indictment reaches its climax: the deepest expression of human depravity is not merely committing evil but approving it in others, thereby creating a culture of rebellion. This corporate dimension of sin sets the stage for Paul's argument that all humanity — Gentile and Jew alike — stands condemned before God (Rom 2-3).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore you are without excuse, O man, every one who judges; for in that which you judge the other, you condemn yourself, for you who judge practice the same things.
verses.note: GREEK: "Anapologētos" (ἀναπολόγητος, Strong's G379) means "without defense, inexcusable." Also used in Rom 1:20. The diatribe style ("O man") is a Stoic rhetorical device Paul adopts, directly addressing an imagined interlocutor who condemns pagan idolatry while practicing similar sins. STRUCTURE: This verse pivots from the indictment of Gentile humanity in 1:18-32 to a universal indictment that includes the moralist and the Jew.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And we know that the judgment of God is according to truth against those who practice such things.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And do you reckon this, O man, the one judging those who practice such things and doing the same, that you will escape the judgment of God?

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Or do you despise the riches of His kindness and forbearance and longsuffering, being ignorant that the kindness of God leads you to repentance?
verses.note: GREEK: "Chrēstotēs" (χρηστότης, G5544) denotes kindness or moral goodness, a near-synonym to grace in function. "Metanoia" (μετάνοια, G3341) means a change of mind/heart, not mere regret. THEOLOGICAL: God's patience is purposive, not permissive—His kindness has a telos (goal), namely repentance. This counters the presumption that delayed judgment equals divine approval.

verses.flow: But according to your hardness and unrepentant heart, you are treasuring up for yourself wrath in the day of wrath and revelation of the righteous judgment of God,
verses.note: GREEK: "Sklērotēs" (σκληρότης, G4643) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, meaning "hardness" or "stubbornness." The cognate verb is used of Pharaoh in the LXX (Ex 7:3). "Dikaiokrisía" (δικαιοκρισία, G1341) is also extremely rare, meaning "righteous judgment," combining díkaios and krísis. THEOLOGICAL: The image of "treasuring up wrath" (thēsaurízō) creates a devastating irony—one stores up not riches but divine wrath through impenitence.

verses.flow: who will render to each one according to his works:
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This is a quotation of Ps 62:12 (LXX 61:13) and Prov 24:12. The principle of recompense according to works is foundational to the biblical theology of judgment from Torah through Revelation (cf. Matt 16:27; Rev 22:12). THEOLOGICAL: Paul establishes that final judgment is according to works—not contradicting justification by faith, but establishing the standard from which all fall short (3:23).

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: to those who by patient endurance in good work seek glory and honor and incorruptibility, life eternal;

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine
verb present passive participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: but to those who are of selfish ambition and who disobey the truth but obey unrighteousness, wrath and fury.

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: Affliction and distress upon every soul of man who works evil, of the Jew first and also of the Greek;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "To the Jew first and also to the Greek" establishes a consistent Pauline principle of covenant priority in both blessing (v.10) and accountability. Israel's election brings greater responsibility, not immunity from judgment. HISTORICAL: "Greek" (Hellēn) functions as a metonym for all non-Jewish peoples in the Greco-Roman world.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: but glory and honor and peace to everyone who works the good, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.
For there is no partiality with God.
verses.note: GREEK: "Prosōpolēmpsia" (προσωπολημψία, G4382) is a distinctly biblical Greek compound meaning "receiving of a face"—i.e., partiality or favoritism. It translates the Hebrew concept of "lifting the face" (nāśā' pānîm). DIVINE_COUNCIL: God as the impartial judge of all humanity is a divine council theme: the true Judge shows no favoritism unlike the corrupt "gods" of Psalm 82:2 who "show partiality to the wicked."

verses.flow: For as many as sinned without the Law shall also perish without the Law, and as many as sinned under the Law shall be judged through the Law.
For not the hearers of the Law are righteous before God, but the doers of the Law shall be justified.
verses.note: GREEK: "Poiētai" (ποιηταί, G4163) means "doers, makers"—those who perform or execute. Cf. James 1:22-25 for the same hearer/doer contrast. THEOLOGICAL: This verse does not teach salvation by works but establishes the legal standard: mere possession of Torah is insufficient. The argument drives toward 3:20 where Paul concludes that no flesh is justified by works of the Law.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural neuter
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For when Gentiles, the ones not having the Law, by nature do the things of the Law, these, not having the Law, are a law to themselves,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "By nature" (phýsei) indicates that Gentiles without Sinai Torah sometimes fulfill its moral substance instinctively—evidence of God's moral law embedded in creation. This anticipates the new covenant promise of the Law written on hearts (Jer 31:33; cf. 2 Cor 3:3). HISTORICAL: This was a significant argument in Second Temple Jewish debates about whether righteous Gentiles could have a share in the world to come.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular feminine
verb present active participle genitive plural masculine
verb present middle participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: who show the work of the Law written in their hearts, their conscience bearing witness together, and their thoughts between one another accusing or even defending them,
in the day when God judges the hidden things of men according to my gospel, through Christ Jesus.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The hidden things of men" (ta krypta tōn anthrōpōn) reveals that divine judgment penetrates beyond external observance to the inner life. The phrase "according to my gospel" connects the day of judgment to the gospel Paul preaches—Christ as eschatological judge is integral to the good news, not separate from it. TYPOLOGY: Christ Jesus as the agent of final judgment fulfills the role assigned to God alone in the OT (Ps 96:13; Eccl 12:14).

verses.flow: But if you call yourself a Jew and rest upon the Law and boast in God,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural neuter
verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and know the will and approve the things that are excellent, being instructed out of the Law,
and have persuaded yourself to be a guide of the blind, a light to those in darkness,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: an instructor of the foolish, a teacher of infants, having the form of knowledge and of the truth in the Law—

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: you then who teach another, do you not teach yourself? You who preach not to steal, do you steal?

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: You who say not to commit adultery, do you commit adultery? You who abhor idols, do you commit sacrilege?
You who boast in the Law, through the transgression of the Law do you dishonor God?
For "the name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you," just as it is written.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Isaiah 52:5 (LXX), originally spoken of Israel's exile causing God's name to be mocked among the nations. The tragic irony persists: those entrusted with God's name bring dishonor upon it by their conduct. HISTORICAL: In the Second Temple period, Jewish communities in the diaspora were highly visible, and moral failures among them were noted by pagan critics such as Juvenal and Tacitus.

verses.flow: For circumcision indeed profits if you practice the Law; but if you are a transgressor of the Law, your circumcision has become uncircumcision.
If therefore the uncircumcised one keeps the righteous requirements of the Law, shall not his uncircumcision be reckoned as circumcision?
verses.note: GREEK: "Dikaiōmata" (δικαιώματα, G1345) means "righteous requirements, ordinances"—the just decrees of the Law. "Logisthēsetai" (λογισθήσεται) from "logízomai" (G3049, to reckon/impute) is a key Pauline term that will become central in chapters 3-4 regarding the imputation of righteousness by faith.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And the one who is by nature uncircumcised, fulfilling the Law, shall judge you who through the letter and circumcision are a transgressor of the Law.
For not the one in the outward is a Jew, nor is circumcision that which is outward in the flesh;
but the one in the hidden is a Jew, and circumcision is of the heart, in the Spirit, not in the letter, whose praise is not from men but from God.
verses.note: GREEK: "Pneuma" (πνεῦμα, G4151) here likely refers to the Holy Spirit as the agent of heart-circumcision, not merely the human spirit—echoing Ezek 36:26-27. "Gramma" (γράμμα, G1121) means the written letter, referring to Torah as external code. TYPOLOGY: "Circumcision of the heart" draws directly from Deut 10:16 and 30:6, and Jer 4:4. Paul identifies the true Jew not by ethnic lineage or physical mark but by inward spiritual transformation—a theme he develops fully in Galatians 3-4 and Philippians 3:3. THEOLOGICAL: The wordplay on "Jew" (Yehudi, from Judah = "praise") is deliberate: the true Jew receives praise (épainos) not from men but from God.

verses.flow: What then is the advantage of the Jew, or what is the profit of circumcision?
Much in every way. First indeed, for that they were entrusted with the oracles of God.
verses.note: GREEK: "Oracles" (λόγια, logia, Strong's G3051) refers to the divine utterances — the Scriptures, covenants, and promises entrusted to Israel. This term carries the weight of authoritative, supernatural speech from God Himself. THEOLOGICAL: Paul affirms that Jewish covenant privilege was real and substantial, not merely ceremonial; Israel was the custodian of God's revealed word (cf. Deut 4:7-8; Ps 147:19-20).

verses.flow: For what if some did not believe? Shall their unbelief nullify the faithfulness of God?
verses.note: GREEK: "Faithfulness" (πίστιν, pistin) of God here means His covenant reliability and trustworthiness, not merely "faith" in the subjective sense. "Nullify" (καταργήσει, katargēsei, Strong's G2673) means to render inoperative or void. THEOLOGICAL: Human unfaithfulness cannot annul the divine covenant commitments — God's promises stand irrevocable (cf. 2 Tim 2:13).

verses.flow: May it never be! But let God be true, and every man a liar, as it has been written: "That You may be justified in Your words, and shall prevail when You are judged."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Psalm 51:4 (LXX 50:6), David's great penitential psalm after his sin with Bathsheba. Paul applies it universally: God is always vindicated as righteous in His judgments. GREEK: "May it never be" (μὴ γένοιτο, mē genoito) is Paul's strongest formula of denial — an optative expressing horrified rejection of a proposition.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But if our unrighteousness commends the righteousness of God, what shall we say? Is God unrighteous who inflicts wrath? I speak according to man.
May it never be! For otherwise, how shall God judge the world?
But if the truth of God abounded through my lie unto His glory, why am I also still judged as a sinner?
And why not — as we are blasphemously reported, and as some affirm that we say — "Let us do evil that good may come"? Whose condemnation is just.
What then? Do we have advantage? Not at all. For we have already charged both Jews and Greeks, that they are all under sin,
as it has been written: "There is none righteous, not even one;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verses 10-18 form a catena — a chain of Old Testament quotations woven together to establish universal human sinfulness. Paul draws from Ps 14:1-3 (LXX 13), Ps 5:9, Ps 140:3, Ps 10:7, Isa 59:7-8, and Ps 36:1. STRUCTURE: This composite indictment moves from the inner person (heart, throat, tongue, lips, mouth) outward to conduct (feet, ways) and concludes with the root cause: no fear of God.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: there is none who understands, there is none who seeks after God.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: All have turned aside, together they have become unprofitable; there is none who does goodness, there is not even one."

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "An opened grave is their throat; with their tongues they practiced deceit." "The venom of asps is under their lips;"
"whose mouth is full of cursing and bitterness."
"Swift are their feet to shed blood;
ruin and misery are in their ways,
and the way of peace they have not known."
"There is no fear of God before their eyes."
Now we know that whatever the Law says, it speaks to those who are under the Law, so that every mouth may be stopped and all the world may become accountable to God.
Because by works of law no flesh shall be justified before Him, for through the law comes knowledge of sin.
verses.note: GREEK: "Flesh" (σάρξ, sarx, Strong's G4561) here means every human being in their creaturely frailty, not merely the physical body. "Knowledge" (ἐπίγνωσις, epignōsis, Strong's G1922) is full, experiential recognition — the law brings thorough awareness of sin, not salvation from it. THEOLOGICAL: This is the definitive statement on the law's inability to justify; it serves a diagnostic, not a redemptive, function (cf. Gal 2:16).

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: But now, apart from law, the righteousness of God has been manifested, being witnessed by the Law and the Prophets,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "But now" (Νυνὶ δέ) marks a decisive eschatological turning point in Paul's argument — from universal condemnation to the revelation of God's saving righteousness apart from law-keeping. TYPOLOGY: "Witnessed by the Law and the Prophets" affirms that the gospel of justification by faith was not a novelty but was anticipated throughout the entire Old Testament witness (cf. Gen 15:6; Hab 2:4; Isa 53).

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: even the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ, unto all who believe; for there is no distinction,
for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God,

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: being justified freely by His grace, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus,

words.m: verb perfect active participle genitive plural neuter

verses.flow: whom God set forth as a propitiation, through faith, in His blood, for a demonstration of His righteousness, on account of the passing over of sins previously committed,
verses.note: GREEK: "Propitiation" (ἱλαστήριον, hilastērion, Strong's G2435) is the same word used in the LXX for the mercy seat — the golden lid of the Ark of the Covenant where sacrificial blood was sprinkled on the Day of Atonement (Lev 16:14-15; Exod 25:17-22). TYPOLOGY: Christ is presented as the true mercy seat — the place where God's wrath is propitiated and His presence meets sinful humanity through atoning blood. The entire tabernacle sacrificial system finds its fulfillment here. DIVINE_COUNCIL: God Himself "set forth" (προέθετο, proetheto — publicly displayed, purposed beforehand) His Son as the locus of divine-human reconciliation, an act decreed in the divine counsel before the ages.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: in the forbearance of God, for the demonstration of His righteousness in the present time, so that He might be just and the justifier of the one who is of faith in Jesus.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The tension Paul resolves is how God can be simultaneously "just" (δίκαιον, dikaion) — upholding His righteous standard — and "the justifier" (δικαιοῦντα, dikaiounta) of sinners who have faith in Jesus. The cross is where divine justice and divine mercy meet without compromise. God's prior forbearance in passing over former sins is now vindicated retrospectively by Christ's sacrifice.

verses.flow: Where then is the boasting? It has been excluded. Through what kind of law? Of works? No, but through a law of faith.
For we reckon that a man is justified by faith, apart from works of law.
verses.note: GREEK: "We reckon" (λογιζόμεθα, logizometha, Strong's G3049) is an accounting term meaning to calculate, consider, or conclude after careful reasoning. This is Paul's summative verdict on the preceding argument. THEOLOGICAL: Justification by faith apart from works of law is not a minor doctrinal point but the central conclusion of Paul's gospel — the article upon which the church stands or falls (cf. Gal 2:16; Phil 3:9).

verses.flow: Or is God the God of Jews only? Is He not also of the Gentiles? Yes, of the Gentiles also,
since indeed God is one, who shall justify the circumcision by faith, and the uncircumcision through faith.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The oneness of God (εἷς ὁ θεός) — the foundational confession of Israel in the Shema (Deut 6:4) — here becomes the very ground for including Gentiles in salvation. If there is one God over all, He must be God of all peoples, and His means of justification must be universal: faith. GREEK: Paul uses two prepositions deliberately — circumcision "by" (ἐκ, ek) faith and uncircumcision "through" (διά, dia) faith — though the distinction is likely rhetorical variation rather than theological difference.

verses.flow: Do we then nullify the law through faith? May it never be! Rather, we establish the law.
verses.note: GREEK: "Establish" (ἱστάνομεν, histanomen, Strong's G2476) means to cause to stand firm, to uphold or confirm. THEOLOGICAL: Far from overthrowing the law, the gospel of justification by faith fulfills the law's deepest intention — to point humanity to its need for God's righteousness. The law's verdict of universal condemnation (vv. 10-20) is precisely what drives humanity to faith, thus vindicating the law's purpose (cf. Rom 8:3-4; Matt 5:17).

verses.flow: What then shall we say that Abraham, our forefather according to the flesh, has found?
For if Abraham was justified by works, he has a boast—but not before God.
For what does the Scripture say? "And Abraham believed God, and it was reckoned to him as righteousness."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Genesis 15:6 (LXX), the foundational declaration of Abraham's justification, which preceded both circumcision (Gen 17) and the giving of the law by centuries. GREEK: ἐλογίσθη (elogisthē, Strong's G3049) is an accounting term meaning "to reckon, credit to one's account." Paul uses this word eleven times in Romans 4, making it the structural keyword of the entire chapter.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now to the one working, the wage is not reckoned according to grace, but according to debt.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative singular masculine
verb present active participle dative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: But to the one not working, yet believing upon the One who justifies the ungodly, his faith is reckoned as righteousness.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is among the most radical declarations in Paul's argument: God justifies τὸν ἀσεβῆ (ton asebē, "the ungodly," Strong's G765). In the OT, justifying the wicked is condemned as an abomination (Prov 17:15), yet God accomplishes what the law forbids through the work of Christ. Faith is not a meritorious work but the instrument by which one receives God's reckoning.

verses.flow: Even as David also speaks of the blessedness of the man to whom God reckons righteousness apart from works:
"Blessed are those whose lawless deeds have been forgiven, and whose sins have been covered;
blessed is the man whose sin the Lord will by no means reckon."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Psalm 32:1-2 (LXX 31:1-2), a psalm of David. David's experience of forgiveness after his sin with Bathsheba becomes a second witness—alongside Abraham—that righteousness is reckoned apart from works. GREEK: κύριος (kyrios) here reflects the LXX rendering of YHWH in the Hebrew Psalm.

verses.flow: This blessedness then—is it upon the circumcision, or also upon the uncircumcision? For we say, "Faith was reckoned to Abraham as righteousness."

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: How then was it reckoned? While he was in circumcision, or in uncircumcision? Not in circumcision, but in uncircumcision.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: And he received the sign of circumcision, a seal of the righteousness of the faith which he had while in uncircumcision, so that he might be father of all those believing through uncircumcision, that righteousness might be reckoned to them,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Circumcision is redefined as σημεῖον (sēmeion, "sign," Strong's G4592) and σφραγῖδα (sphragida, "seal," Strong's G4973)—a confirmation of a righteousness already received, not a means of obtaining it. HISTORICAL: This was profoundly counter-cultural in Second Temple Judaism, where circumcision was the covenant marker par excellence (cf. Jubilees 15:25-34).

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: and father of circumcision to those who are not of circumcision only, but who also walk in the footsteps of the faith of our father Abraham, which he had while in uncircumcision.
For not through the law was the promise to Abraham or to his seed—that he should be heir of the world—but through the righteousness of faith.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The promise that Abraham would be κληρονόμον κόσμου ("heir of the world") is not stated verbatim in Genesis but is Paul's theological summary of Gen 12:1-3, Gen 15:5, and Gen 17:4-6, read through the lens of the Abrahamic covenant's ultimate scope. This cosmic inheritance finds its fulfillment in Christ (Gal 3:16, 29).

verses.flow: For if those of the law are heirs, faith has been made void and the promise has been nullified.
For the law works wrath; but where there is no law, neither is there transgression.
verses.note: GREEK: κατεργάζεται (katergazetai, Strong's G2716) means "works out, produces, brings about." The law, by defining transgression (παράβασις, parabasis, Strong's G3847), produces the awareness and juridical reality of wrath. This is a key link in Paul's argument: law without faith yields condemnation, not inheritance.

verses.flow: For this reason it is of faith, that it might be according to grace, so that the promise might be firm to all the seed—not to that of the law only, but also to that of the faith of Abraham, who is father of us all,

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verb present active participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: as it is written, "A father of many nations I have appointed you"—before the God whom he believed, the One making alive the dead and calling the things not being as being.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Genesis 17:5 (LXX). Paul frames Abraham's faith as trust in the God who ζῳοποιοῦντος τοὺς νεκρούς ("makes alive the dead")—an anticipation of resurrection theology. Abraham's "dead" body and Sarah's "dead" womb foreshadow the ultimate life-from-death: the resurrection of Jesus (v. 24-25). DIVINE_COUNCIL: God's sovereign power over life and death—calling τὰ μὴ ὄντα ὡς ὄντα ("the things not being as being")—reflects His unique creative prerogative as the Most High.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: Who against hope, upon hope believed, so that he became father of many nations, according to that which had been spoken, "So shall your seed be."
