verses.flow: And not having grown weak in faith, he considered his own body, already having been made dead, being about a hundred years old, and the deadness of the womb of Sarah.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Yet regarding the promise of God, he did not waver in unbelief, but was empowered by faith, having given glory to God,
verses.note: GREEK: διεκρίθη (diekrithē, Strong's G1252) means "to be divided, to waver, to be at odds with oneself." Abraham did not stand in internal division between trust and distrust. ἐνεδυναμώθη (enedynamōthē, Strong's G1743) is a divine passive—"was empowered"—suggesting God Himself strengthened Abraham's faith.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and having been fully persuaded that what He had promised, He is able also to do.
Therefore also it was reckoned to him as righteousness.
Now it was not written for his sake alone that it was reckoned to him,

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: but also for our sake, to whom it is about to be reckoned—to those believing upon the One who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead,
who was delivered over on account of our trespasses and was raised on account of our justification.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is a carefully balanced creedal formula. παρεδόθη (paredothē, "was delivered over") echoes Isaiah 53:12 (LXX παρεδόθη), identifying Jesus with the Suffering Servant. The parallel structure—delivered on account of (διά + accusative) trespasses, raised on account of (διά + accusative) justification—ties atonement and resurrection together as inseparable realities. STRUCTURE: This verse forms the climactic conclusion to Romans 4 and the theological bridge into Romans 5:1, where the fruit of justification by faith is expounded.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Having been justified, therefore, out of faith, we have peace toward God through our Lord Jesus Christ,
verses.note: GREEK: "Dikaiōthentes" (δικαιωθέντες, Strong's G1344) is an aorist passive participle of dikaioō, meaning "having been declared righteous." The passive voice indicates God as the acting agent. The textual variant between echomen (indicative, "we have") and echōmen (subjunctive, "let us have") is significant; the indicative is preferred, declaring peace as an accomplished reality of justification. THEOLOGICAL: Peace (eirēnē) here is not subjective feeling but objective restored relationship with God—the cessation of divine enmity toward those justified by faith (Rom 1:18; 8:1).

verses.flow: through whom also we have obtained access by faith into this grace in which we stand, and we boast upon hope of the glory of God.
verses.note: GREEK: "Prosagōgē" (προσαγωγή, Strong's G4318) means "access" or "introduction," a term used for being ushered into the presence of a king. It occurs only three times in the NT (also Eph 2:18; 3:12). "Eschēkamen" (perfect tense) indicates a permanent standing gained. THEOLOGICAL: Believers stand in grace as a present, settled state—not a precarious position but a realm of divine favor accessed through Christ as mediator.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And not only so, but we also boast in tribulations, knowing that tribulation works endurance,
and endurance, proven character, and proven character, hope,

words.m: verb aorist passive participle genitive singular neuter

verses.flow: and hope does not put to shame, because the love of God has been poured out in our hearts through the Holy Spirit who was given to us.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ekkechytai" (ἐκκέχυται, Strong's G1632) is a perfect passive of ekcheō, "to pour out," indicating a past action with enduring results. The love of God is not merely known but experientially lavished within the heart. THEOLOGICAL: This is the first explicit mention of the Holy Spirit in Romans' justification argument. The Spirit's indwelling is the guarantee that eschatological hope will not disappoint—an echo of Joel 2:28-29 and the promise of the new covenant (Jer 31:33). TYPOLOGY: The "pouring out" language recalls the Pentecost event (Acts 2:17-18, 33), connecting justification to the inaugurated age of the Spirit.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: For while we were yet weak, still at the appointed time, Christ died on behalf of the ungodly.
For scarcely on behalf of a righteous man will someone die; for on behalf of the good man perhaps someone even dares to die.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: But God demonstrates his own love toward us, in that while we were yet sinners, Christ died on our behalf.
verses.note: GREEK: "Synistēsin" (συνίστησιν, Strong's G4921) means "demonstrates," "commends," or "establishes together"—God sets forth proof of His love, not merely declares it. The present tense suggests God continually demonstrates this love. THEOLOGICAL: The timing is critical: "while we were yet sinners" (eti hamartōlōn ontōn hēmōn). God's love is not responsive to human merit but initiatory and unconditional, forming the ground of assurance for believers.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Much more, therefore, having been justified now by his blood, we shall be saved through him from the wrath.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For if, being enemies, we were reconciled to God through the death of his Son, much more, having been reconciled, we shall be saved by his life.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul employs a qal wahomer (lesser-to-greater) argument: if reconciliation came through Christ's death when we were enemies, how much more shall his resurrection life secure our final salvation. The distinction between Christ's death (reconciliation) and Christ's life (ongoing salvation) is foundational to understanding the scope of redemption. GREEK: "Katallagentes" (καταλλαγέντες, Strong's G2644) is aorist passive—reconciliation is a completed divine act, not a human achievement.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And not only so, but also boasting in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have now received the reconciliation.
On account of this, just as through one man sin entered into the world, and through sin, death, and so death spread to all men, because all sinned—
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse introduces the Adam-Christ typology, the most extensive federal headship argument in Scripture. Adam functions as representative head whose single act brought sin and death to all humanity. The phrase "eph' hō pantes hēmarton" (because all sinned) has been debated for centuries—it likely means all sinned "in Adam" representatively, explaining universal death. TYPOLOGY: Adam is the anti-type whose failure sets the stage for the "one to come" (v. 14)—Christ as the last Adam (1 Cor 15:45) whose single act reverses the catastrophe. STRUCTURE: The sentence begun here is grammatically incomplete (anacoluthon)—Paul breaks off the comparison and does not complete the "so also" clause until v. 18.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: for until the law, sin was in the world, but sin is not reckoned when there is no law.

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative plural masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: But death reigned from Adam until Moses, even over those who had not sinned in the likeness of the transgression of Adam, who is a type of the one to come.
verses.note: GREEK: "Typos" (τύπος, Strong's G5179) means "pattern," "figure," or "prefiguration." Adam is explicitly called a type of "the one to come" (tou mellontos), identifying Christ. This is one of the clearest typological statements in the NT. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Death "reigning" (ebasileusen) personifies it as a cosmic power—a rival king exercising dominion over humanity. Christ's work overthrows this illegitimate reign (cf. 1 Cor 15:25-26; Heb 2:14-15).

verses.flow: But not as the trespass, so also is the free gift. For if by the trespass of the one the many died, much more the grace of God and the gift by the grace of the one man, Jesus Christ, abounded to the many.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: And not as through one having sinned is the gift; for the judgment was from one trespass unto condemnation, but the free gift is from many trespasses unto a righteous decree.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For if by the trespass of the one, death reigned through the one, much more those who receive the abundance of grace and of the gift of righteousness shall reign in life through the one, Jesus Christ.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The climax of the Adam-Christ contrast: whereas through Adam death reigned over humanity as subjects, through Christ believers do not merely escape death's reign but themselves "reign in life." The shift from passive subjection to active royal participation is stunning—believers become co-regents with Christ (2 Tim 2:12; Rev 5:10). GREEK: "Perisseian" (περισσείαν, Strong's G4050) means "abundance" or "surplus"—grace is not merely equal to the trespass but overflows beyond it.

verses.flow: So then, as through one trespass unto all men unto condemnation, so also through one righteous act unto all men unto justification of life.
For just as through the disobedience of the one man the many were appointed sinners, so also through the obedience of the one the many shall be appointed righteous.
verses.note: GREEK: "Katestathēsan" (κατεστάθησαν, Strong's G2525) means "were constituted" or "were appointed to a position"—not merely "made" in a transformative sense but legally established in a category. The many were constituted sinners by Adam's disobedience; the many shall be constituted righteous by Christ's obedience. TYPOLOGY: Christ's obedience (hypakoē) reverses Adam's disobedience (parakoē)—note the deliberate wordplay with the shared root akoē ("hearing"). Where Adam refused to hear God's command (Gen 2:17), Christ was obedient unto death (Phil 2:8).

verses.flow: And the law came in alongside, so that the trespass might abound; but where sin abounded, grace super-abounded,
verses.note: GREEK: "Pareisēlthen" (παρεισῆλθεν, Strong's G3922) means "came in alongside" or "entered by a side road"—the law was not the main act in salvation history but an auxiliary entrance. "Hyperperisseusen" (ὑπερεπερίσσευσεν, Strong's G5248) is a rare, intensified compound verb meaning "super-abounded" or "overflowed beyond all measure"—a hapax legomenon in the Pauline corpus (cf. similar form in 2 Cor 7:4). THEOLOGICAL: The law's purpose was not to remedy sin but to expose and multiply it, thereby magnifying the need for grace. This is one of Paul's most provocative claims about Torah (cf. Gal 3:19).

verses.flow: so that, just as sin reigned in death, so also grace might reign through righteousness unto eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chapter concludes with the reign of grace as a cosmic counter-kingdom to the reign of sin. Grace rules "through righteousness"—not bypassing justice but operating through it, because Christ's atoning work satisfied divine justice. The goal is "eternal life" (zōēn aiōnion), the eschatological life of the age to come, mediated "through Jesus Christ our Lord"—the same formula that opened the chapter (v. 1), forming an inclusio. STRUCTURE: The phrase "through Jesus Christ our Lord" bookends vv. 1-21, establishing Christ as the sole mediator of every blessing enumerated in the chapter.

verses.flow: What then shall we say? Shall we continue in sin, that grace might abound?
May it never be! We who died to sin, how shall we still live in it?
verses.note: GREEK: μὴ γένοιτο (mē genoito) is an aorist optative expressing the strongest possible negation in Greek — "May it never come to be!" Paul uses this phrase frequently in Romans as a rhetorical device to reject false conclusions drawn from his arguments (Rom 3:4, 3:6, 3:31, 7:7, 9:14, 11:1). The phrase conveys visceral horror at the suggestion.

verses.flow: Or are you ignorant that as many as were baptized into Christ Jesus, into His death were baptized?
We were buried together therefore with Him through baptism into death, that just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, so also we might walk in newness of life.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Baptism is presented as a typological participation in Christ's death, burial, and resurrection. The descent into water corresponds to burial (συνετάφημεν, syntaphēmen — "buried together"), and emergence corresponds to resurrection. GREEK: καινότητι ζωῆς (kainotēti zōēs, "newness of life") — kainotēs (G2538) denotes qualitative newness, not merely chronological newness (neos), indicating an entirely different quality of existence.

verses.flow: For if we have become planted together in the likeness of His death, certainly also we shall be of the resurrection,
verses.note: GREEK: σύμφυτοι (symphytoi, G4854) — a hapax legomenon in the NT, literally "grown together, planted together." The term is botanical, evoking the image of a graft that becomes organically united with the host plant. TYPOLOGY: This organic union with Christ in death guarantees organic union in resurrection — the pattern of the firstfruits offering (Lev 23:10-11; 1 Cor 15:20).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: knowing this, that our old man was crucified together with Him, that the body of sin might be rendered powerless, so that we should no longer be enslaved to sin;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: ὁ παλαιὸς ἡμῶν ἄνθρωπος (ho palaios hēmōn anthrōpos, "our old man") refers not merely to former habits but to the entire Adamic identity under the dominion of sin (cf. Rom 5:12-21). GREEK: καταργηθῇ (katargēthē) means "rendered inoperative, made powerless" (G2673) — the body of sin is not annihilated but dethroned, stripped of its ruling authority.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: for the one who has died has been justified from sin.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: δεδικαίωται ἀπὸ τῆς ἁμαρτίας (dedikaiōtai apo tēs hamartias) — "has been justified from sin." This reflects a legal principle also found in rabbinic teaching (b. Shabbat 151b): death pays the debt, releasing the deceased from sin's legal claim. The believer's co-death with Christ thus constitutes a judicial settlement.

verses.flow: And if we died with Christ, we believe that we shall also live together with Him,

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: knowing that Christ, having been raised from the dead, no longer dies; death no longer has dominion over Him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: θάνατος αὐτοῦ οὐκέτι κυριεύει (thanatos autou ouketi kyrieuei) — "death no longer has dominion over Him." GREEK: κυριεύει (kyrieuei, G2961) is cognate with κύριος (kyrios, "Lord"). Paul employs deliberate irony: death once appeared to "lord it over" the Lord, but Christ's resurrection overturned death's pretended sovereignty permanently. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Death functioned as a hostile power in the cosmic order; Christ's resurrection constitutes His decisive victory over this enemy (1 Cor 15:26).

verses.flow: For the death that He died, He died to sin once for all; and the life that He lives, He lives to God.
verses.note: GREEK: ἐφάπαξ (ephapax, G2178) — "once for all." This term emphasizes the unrepeatable, decisive, and sufficient nature of Christ's atoning death. It stands in deliberate contrast to the repeated sacrifices of the Levitical system (cf. Heb 7:27, 9:12, 10:10). The dative τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ indicates Christ died "with reference to sin" — not as a sinner, but dealing conclusively with sin's claim.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: So also you, reckon yourselves to be dead indeed to sin, but alive to God in Christ Jesus.
Therefore let not sin reign in your mortal body, so as to obey its desires.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Neither present your members as instruments of unrighteousness to sin, but present yourselves to God as those alive from the dead, and your members as instruments of righteousness to God.
For sin shall not have dominion over you, for you are not under law but under grace.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The promise that sin shall not have dominion (κυριεύσει, kyrieusei) is grounded not in human effort but in the believer's transfer from the sphere of law to the sphere of grace. STRUCTURE: This verse serves as a hinge — concluding the indicative section (vv. 1-14a) and introducing the imperative discussion (vv. 15-23). Paul's logic: the ground of sanctification is the same as the ground of justification — grace, not law.

verses.flow: What then? Shall we sin because we are not under law but under grace? May it never be!
Do you not know that to whom you present yourselves as slaves for obedience, slaves you are to the one whom you obey, whether of sin unto death, or of obedience unto righteousness?
But thanks be to God, that you were slaves of sin, yet you obeyed from the heart the pattern of teaching to which you were delivered,
verses.note: GREEK: τύπον διδαχῆς (typon didachēs) — "pattern of teaching" or "form of doctrine." τύπος (typos, G5179) means a mold or stamp that imprints a shape. Unusually, Paul says the believers were "delivered to" (παρεδόθητε, paredothēte) the teaching, rather than the teaching delivered to them — the gospel pattern shapes and molds the believer, not the reverse.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and having been set free from sin, you were enslaved to righteousness.

I speak in human terms on account of the weakness of your flesh. For just as you presented your members as slaves to uncleanness and to lawlessness unto lawlessness, so now present your members as slaves to righteousness unto sanctification.
For when you were slaves of sin, you were free in regard to righteousness.
What fruit then did you have at that time, in the things of which you are now ashamed? For the end of those things is death.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But now, having been set free from sin and having been enslaved to God, you have your fruit unto sanctification, and the end, eternal life.
For the wages of sin is death, but the gracious gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.
verses.note: GREEK: ὀψώνια (opsōnia, G3800) originally denoted a soldier's rations or wages — a military term. Sin is thus portrayed as a commanding officer who pays its soldiers with death. THEOLOGICAL: Paul sets up a deliberate asymmetry: sin pays "wages" (something earned), but God gives a "gracious gift" (χάρισμα, charisma, G5486) — eternal life is never earned but freely bestowed. The closing phrase "in Christ Jesus our Lord" anchors the entire chapter's argument: union with Christ is the sphere in which this gift is received and enjoyed.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: Or are you ignorant, brothers — for I speak to those knowing the law — that the law has dominion over a man for as long a time as he lives?

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the married woman has been bound by law to the living husband; but if the husband should die, she has been released from the law of the husband.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: So then, while the husband is living, she shall be called an adulteress if she becomes joined to another man; but if the husband should die, she is free from the law, so as not to be an adulteress having become joined to another man.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: So then, my brothers, you also were put to death to the law through the body of Christ, so that you might become joined to another — to the One having been raised from the dead — in order that we might bear fruit to God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is the hinge of Paul's marriage analogy. Believers did not merely have the law die; they themselves "were put to death" (ἐθανατώθητε, aorist passive) to the law through Christ's crucified body. The purpose clause (εἰς τό + infinitive) reveals the goal: union with the risen Christ, producing fruit for God. TYPOLOGY: The marriage imagery echoes Hosea's covenant-marriage motif (Hos 2:19-20) and anticipates the Bride of Christ theme (Eph 5:25-27; Rev 19:7).

verses.flow: For when we were in the flesh, the passions of sins, those through the law, were working in our members so as to bear fruit to death.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But now we have been released from the law, having died to that in which we were held, so that we serve in newness of the Spirit and not in oldness of the letter.
verses.note: GREEK: "Newness" (καινότητι, kainotēs, Strong's G2538) and "oldness" (παλαιότητι, palaiotēs, G3821) are both rare in the NT. Paul contrasts two modes of service: the Spirit (πνεύματος) versus the letter (γράμματος). THEOLOGICAL: This does not diminish the law's holiness (cf. v.12) but distinguishes the old covenant's written code from the Spirit's empowering presence under the new covenant (2 Cor 3:6; Ezek 36:26-27).

verses.flow: What then shall we say? Is the law sin? May it never be! But I did not know sin except through the law; for indeed I would not have known covetousness except the law was saying, "You shall not covet."
verses.note: GREEK: "You shall not covet" (Οὐκ ἐπιθυμήσεις) directly quotes the LXX of Exodus 20:17 / Deuteronomy 5:21 (Tenth Commandment). The verb ἐπιθυμέω (G1937) encompasses all illicit desire, not merely sexual lust. THEOLOGICAL: Paul selects this commandment because it uniquely targets internal disposition rather than outward act, demonstrating the law's capacity to expose the heart's rebellion. The diatribe form ("What then shall we say?") is characteristic of Romans' rhetorical structure (cf. Rom 3:5; 6:1).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: But sin, having received opportunity through the commandment, produced in me every manner of covetousness; for apart from the law, sin is dead.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀφορμή (aphormē, G874) means "base of operations" or "occasion" — a military term for a staging ground. Sin used the commandment as its operational base to produce (κατειργάσατο, katergázomai) every covetousness. THEOLOGICAL: The commandment is not the cause of sin but the occasion sin exploits. Apart from the law, sin is "dead" — not non-existent but dormant and unrecognized.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: And I was once alive apart from the law, but when the commandment came, sin came to life again,
and I died; and the commandment, the one unto life, this was found to me to be unto death.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: For sin, having received opportunity through the commandment, deceived me, and through it put me to death.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The language of deception (ἐξηπάτησέν, exēpatēsen, G1818) echoes Genesis 3:13 LXX where Eve says the serpent "deceived me" (ἠπάτησέν με). Paul portrays sin as a personal agent that, like the serpent, uses God's own command as the instrument of deception and death. The commandment, like the tree of knowledge, was good but became the occasion of the fall.

verses.flow: So then, the law indeed is holy, and the commandment is holy and righteous and good.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: Did then that which is good become death to me? May it never be! But sin, in order that it might be shown to be sin, was working death in me through that which is good — in order that sin might become sinful beyond measure through the commandment.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For we know that the law is spiritual, but I am fleshly, having been sold under sin.
verses.note: GREEK: "Fleshly" (σάρκινος, sarkinos, G4560) denotes "made of flesh" — material composition — whereas σαρκικός (sarkikos) would emphasize fleshly behavior. "Having been sold" (πεπραμένος, perfect passive participle of πιπράσκω, G4097) depicts the state of slavery to sin, echoing the OT sale of persons into bondage (1 Kgs 21:20, 25 LXX). THEOLOGICAL: The shift to present-tense verbs from v.14 onward has generated extensive debate over whether Paul describes his pre-conversion or ongoing Christian experience.

verses.flow: For what I am working out, I do not understand; for not what I will, this I practice, but what I hate, this I do.
But if what I do not will, this I do, I agree with the law that it is good.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: But now it is no longer I working it out, but sin dwelling in me.
For I know that there does not dwell in me — that is, in my flesh — any good thing; for the willing is present with me, but the working out of the good is not.
For the good that I will, I do not do; but the evil that I do not will, this I practice.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: But if what I do not will, this I do, it is no longer I working it out, but sin dwelling in me.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: I find then the law: that when I will to do the good, evil is present with me.
For I delight in the law of God according to the inner man;
verses.note: GREEK: συνήδομαι (synēdomai, G4913) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "I delight together with" or "I rejoice with." The "inner man" (τὸν ἔσω ἄνθρωπον) refers to the renewed mind or spirit as distinct from the flesh (cf. 2 Cor 4:16; Eph 3:16). THEOLOGICAL: This verse demonstrates that the struggle Paul describes is not one of total depravity but of a divided will — the regenerate self genuinely delighting in God's law while the flesh rebels.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular masculine
verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: but I see another law in my members, warring against the law of my mind, and taking me captive to the law of sin which is in my members.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "law of my mind" (τῷ νόμῳ τοῦ νοός μου) stands in cosmic conflict with the "law of sin" in the members — language reflecting the spiritual warfare framework where human beings are the battleground between divine order and rebellious powers (cf. Eph 6:12). GREEK: ἀντιστρατευόμενον (antistrateuomenon, G497) is a military compound verb: "warring against in opposition," depicting two armies in pitched battle within the self. αἰχμαλωτίζοντα (aichmalōtizonta, G163) means "taking captive at spear-point."

verses.flow: Wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death?
verses.note: GREEK: ταλαίπωρος (talaipōros, G5005) means "wretched, miserable" and appears only here and in Rev 3:17. "Body of this death" (τοῦ σώματος τοῦ θανάτου τούτου) treats the physical body as the locus of sin's indwelling power. HISTORICAL: Some scholars connect this imagery to an ancient punishment in which a corpse was bound to a living prisoner — though this is debated, the visceral horror of the image is unmistakable.

verses.flow: Thanks be to God, through Jesus Christ our Lord! So then, I myself with the mind indeed serve the law of God, but with the flesh the law of sin.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The cry of v.24 receives its answer: deliverance comes through Jesus Christ the Lord — not through the law, not through human effort. The concluding summary encapsulates the entire chapter's argument: the mind (νοΐ) serves God's law, the flesh (σαρκί) serves sin's law. This tension is resolved not here but in Romans 8:1-4, where the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus sets the believer free. STRUCTURE: Romans 7 functions as the bridge between the believer's death to sin (ch. 6) and life in the Spirit (ch. 8).

verses.flow: There is therefore now no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Greek katákrima (κατάκριμα, Strong's G2631) refers not merely to the act of condemning but to the sentence of condemnation — the judicial verdict and its penalty. Paul's "therefore" (ἄρα) draws the conclusion from the entire argument of Romans 5-7: the believer's union with Christ has annulled the condemning sentence. This verse forms the theological hinge of the entire epistle.

verses.flow: For the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus has set you free from the law of sin and of death.
verses.note: GREEK: "Law" (νόμος, nomos) is used in two contrasting senses: the governing principle of the Spirit that gives life versus the governing principle of sin that produces death. The verb ἠλευθέρωσεν (ēleuthérōsen) is aorist indicative, indicating a decisive, completed act of liberation. Some manuscripts read "me" (με) rather than "you" (σε).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For what the law could not do, in that it was weak through the flesh, God, having sent His own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh and concerning sin, condemned sin in the flesh,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The phrase περὶ ἁμαρτίας (peri hamartias, "concerning sin") echoes the LXX language for the sin offering (Lev 4:3; Isa 53:10), suggesting Christ's death functions as the ultimate sin offering. "In the likeness of sinful flesh" (ἐν ὁμοιώματι σαρκὸς ἁμαρτίας) preserves Christ's full humanity while distinguishing Him from sinful humanity. God accomplished what Torah could not by sending His own Son as both priest and offering.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: in order that the righteous requirement of the law might be fulfilled in us, who walk not according to the flesh but according to the Spirit.
verses.note: GREEK: δικαίωμα (dikaíōma, Strong's G1345) here means the "righteous requirement" or "just ordinance" of the law — its legitimate demand. The passive πληρωθῇ (plērōthē, "might be fulfilled") indicates that this fulfillment is God's work in believers, not their own achievement. The walk (περιπατέω) according to the Spirit is the evidence, not the cause, of this fulfillment.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For those who are according to the flesh set their minds on the things of the flesh, but those who are according to the Spirit, on the things of the Spirit.
For the mind of the flesh is death, but the mind of the Spirit is life and peace;
because the mind of the flesh is enmity toward God, for it does not subject itself to the law of God, for neither is it able to do so.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And those who are in the flesh are not able to please God.
But you are not in the flesh but in the Spirit, if indeed the Spirit of God dwells in you. But if anyone does not have the Spirit of Christ, this one is not His.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse contains one of the most explicit Trinitarian formulations in Paul: "Spirit of God" and "Spirit of Christ" are used interchangeably, establishing the inseparability of the divine persons in the economy of salvation. The conditional εἴπερ ("if indeed") assumes the reality of the Spirit's indwelling for genuine believers. To lack the Spirit of Christ is to not belong to Him (αὐτοῦ).

verses.flow: But if Christ is in you, the body indeed is dead because of sin, but the Spirit is life because of righteousness.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And if the Spirit of the One who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you, the One who raised Christ Jesus from the dead shall also make alive your mortal bodies through His Spirit who dwells in you.
So then, brothers, we are debtors, not to the flesh, to live according to the flesh.
For if you live according to the flesh, you are about to die; but if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you shall live.
For as many as are led by the Spirit of God, these are sons of God.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase "sons of God" (υἱοὶ θεοῦ, huioi theou) carries immense theological weight. In the OT and Second Temple literature, "sons of God" (bene elohim) designated divine council members (Job 1:6; Ps 82:6; Dan 3:25 LXX). Paul radically applies this status to Spirit-led believers, signifying their exalted position in the divine family — a status forfeited by rebellious spiritual powers (Ps 82) and now granted to those in Christ.

verses.flow: For you did not receive a spirit of bondage again unto fear, but you received the Spirit of adoption, by whom we cry out, "Abba! Father!"
verses.note: GREEK: υἱοθεσία (huiothesia, Strong's G5206) is a Greco-Roman legal term for adoption, granting full rights of inheritance. Paul contrasts it with δουλεία (douleia, "bondage/slavery"). The Aramaic "Abba" (Αββα) reflects the intimate address Jesus Himself used for the Father (Mark 14:36), preserved here untranslated alongside the Greek ὁ πατήρ. HISTORICAL: Adoption in Roman law was irrevocable and granted the adopted son the full legal status of a natural-born heir.

verses.flow: The Spirit Himself bears witness together with our spirit that we are children of God.
And if children, also heirs — heirs indeed of God, and fellow-heirs with Christ — if indeed we suffer together with Him, in order that we may also be glorified together.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul establishes the golden chain: children → heirs → fellow-heirs with Christ. The compound συγκληρονόμοι (synklēronomoi, "fellow-heirs") places believers in inheritance alongside the Son Himself. The conditional εἴπερ ("if indeed") links suffering with glory — not as merit but as the pattern of Christ's own path to exaltation (Phil 2:8-9). Suffering with Christ is the necessary antecedent to glorification.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: For I reckon that the sufferings of the present time are not worthy to be compared with the glory about to be revealed unto us.
For the earnest expectation of the creation eagerly awaits the revelation of the sons of God.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀποκαραδοκία (apokaradokia, Strong's G603) is a vivid compound word meaning "to stretch the head forward eagerly" — an intense, anxious watching. κτίσις (ktisis) here refers to the entire created order, not merely humanity. TYPOLOGY: This echoes the Edenic mandate; creation awaits the full restoration that the "sons of God" will bring when they are revealed in glory, reversing the curse of Genesis 3:17-19.

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the creation was subjected to futility, not willingly, but on account of the one who subjected it, in hope
that the creation itself also shall be set free from the bondage of corruption into the freedom of the glory of the children of God.
For we know that all the creation groans together and travails together until now.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And not only so, but also we ourselves, having the firstfruits of the Spirit, even we ourselves groan within ourselves, eagerly awaiting adoption, the redemption of our body.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀπαρχή (aparchē, "firstfruits," Strong's G536) is a sacrificial/agricultural term from the LXX (Lev 23:10). The Spirit's present indwelling is the firstfruits — the initial portion guaranteeing the full harvest of bodily resurrection. The "adoption" (υἱοθεσία) here is the future, consummated aspect: the redemption (ἀπολύτρωσις) of the physical body, not merely a present legal status.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: For in hope we were saved; but hope that is seen is not hope, for what one sees, why does he yet hope for it?
But if we hope for what we do not see, through patient endurance we eagerly await it.
And likewise also the Spirit helps our weakness; for what we should pray for as it is necessary, we do not know, but the Spirit Himself intercedes with groanings unutterable.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the One who searches the hearts knows what the mind of the Spirit is, because He intercedes for the saints according to God.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine
verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: And we know that for those who love God, all things work together for good, for those who are called according to His purpose.
verses.note: GREEK: συνεργεῖ (synergei, "works together") has a textual variant — some manuscripts include ὁ θεός as the subject ("God works all things together"), while others leave "all things" as the subject. The phrase κατὰ πρόθεσιν (kata prothesin, "according to purpose") denotes God's sovereign, predetermined plan. THEOLOGICAL: This verse introduces the great chain of salvation (vv. 29-30), grounding the believer's assurance not in their own love for God but in God's eternal purpose.

verses.flow: For those whom He foreknew, He also predetermined to be conformed to the image of His Son, so that He might be the firstborn among many brothers.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: προέγνω (proegnō, "foreknew") carries the Hebraic sense of intimate, covenantal knowledge (cf. Amos 3:2; Gen 18:19 LXX), not mere prescience. προώρισεν (proōrisen, "predetermined") establishes God's sovereign decree. The goal is conformity to the εἰκών (eikōn, "image") of the Son — the restoration of the divine image lost in Adam (Gen 1:26-27). Christ as πρωτότοκος ("firstborn") holds preeminence among a vast family of redeemed brothers.

verses.flow: And those whom He predetermined, these He also called; and those whom He called, these He also justified; and those whom He justified, these He also glorified.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: This verse contains the famous "golden chain" (catena aurea) of salvation: foreordained → called → justified → glorified. The aorist tense of ἐδόξασεν (edoxasen, "glorified") is remarkable — Paul speaks of future glorification as already accomplished, so certain is it in the divine purpose. Each link is unbreakable; no one is lost between stages.

verses.flow: What then shall we say to these things? If God is for us, who is against us?
He who indeed did not spare His own Son, but delivered Him up for us all, how shall He not also, together with Him, freely give us all things?

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Who shall bring a charge against the chosen ones of God? God is the One who justifies.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Who is the one who condemns? Christ is the One who died, and more than that, who was raised, who also is at the right hand of God, who also intercedes for us.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul's rhetorical defense echoes the courtroom language of Isaiah 50:8-9 ("Who shall contend with me?"). Christ's fourfold work is enumerated: died, was raised, is at the right hand of God, intercedes. The "right hand of God" (ἐν δεξιᾷ τοῦ θεοῦ) alludes to Psalm 110:1, the most quoted OT passage in the NT, establishing Christ's royal-priestly session.

verses.flow: Who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Shall tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or peril, or sword?
As it is written: "For Your sake we are put to death all the day long; we were accounted as sheep for slaughter."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Psalm 44:22 (LXX 43:23), a psalm of communal lament where Israel suffers not because of unfaithfulness but precisely because of covenant loyalty. Paul applies this to the church: suffering is the normative condition of God's people in the present age, not evidence of divine abandonment.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: But in all these things we more than conquer through the One who loved us.
verses.note: GREEK: ὑπερνικῶμεν (hypernikōmen, Strong's G5245) is a Pauline hapax legomenon — "we hyper-conquer" or "we overwhelmingly conquer." The ὑπερ- prefix intensifies beyond ordinary victory. The present tense indicates ongoing, continuous triumph. Victory comes not from the believers' own strength but "through the One who loved us" (διὰ τοῦ ἀγαπήσαντος ἡμᾶς).

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural neuter
verb present active participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: For I am persuaded that neither death nor life, nor angels nor principalities, nor things present nor things to come, nor powers,
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Angels" (ἄγγελοι) and "principalities" (ἀρχαί) and "powers" (δυνάμεις) reference the spiritual hierarchy of the divine council and cosmic powers — both loyal and rebellious. Paul asserts that no being in the entire spiritual realm, regardless of rank or allegiance, can sever believers from God's love. This language reflects Second Temple Jewish angelology and the Deuteronomy 32 worldview of nations under spiritual authorities (cf. Dan 10:13, 20; Eph 6:12).

verses.flow: nor height nor depth, nor any other created thing, shall be able to separate us from the love of God which is in Christ Jesus our Lord.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: ὕψωμα (hypsōma, "height") and βάθος (bathos, "depth") may reflect astrological terminology for the highest and lowest points of celestial bodies, thus encompassing every dimension of the cosmos. Paul's climactic declaration seals the chapter: no power in all creation can separate believers from God's love, which is localized and mediated "in Christ Jesus our Lord" (ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ τῷ κυρίῳ ἡμῶν) — the phrase that opened the chapter (v. 1) now closes it in magnificent inclusio.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: I speak truth in Christ, I do not lie, my conscience bearing witness with me in the Holy Spirit,
that grief is to me great, and unceasing pain in my heart.
For I was wishing myself to be accursed from Christ on behalf of my brothers, my kinsmen according to the flesh,
verses.note: GREEK: "I was wishing" (ēuchomēn, G2172) is an imperfect middle, expressing an unrealizable wish—Paul contemplates the impossible hypothetical of being anathema (G331, "accursed," a term for something devoted to destruction) separated from Christ for the sake of his Jewish kinsmen. THEOLOGICAL: This echoes Moses' intercession in Exodus 32:32, where Moses offered to be blotted from God's book for Israel's sake.

verses.flow: who are Israelites, whose is the adoption, and the glory, and the covenants, and the giving of the law, and the service, and the promises,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul lists seven covenant privileges belonging to Israel: adoption (huiothesia, G5206), glory (the Shekinah presence), covenants (Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic), the giving of the law (nomothesia, G3548, hapax in NT), the temple service (latreia), and the promises. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "glory" (doxa) refers to the visible manifestation of God's kavod dwelling among Israel, the enthroned divine presence.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: whose are the fathers, and from whom is the Christ according to the flesh, who is over all, God blessed unto the ages. Amen.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse contains one of the strongest affirmations of Christ's deity in Paul's letters. The phrase "who is over all, God blessed unto the ages" (ho ōn epi pantōn theos eulogētos) most naturally applies to Christ as its grammatical subject. GREEK: Eulogētos (G2128) is used exclusively of God in the LXX and NT doxologies, making its application to Christ a remarkable theological claim.

verses.flow: But it is not as though the word of God has fallen. For not all those from Israel are Israel;
verses.note: STRUCTURE: This verse pivots the argument from Paul's lament (vv. 1-5) to his theological defense of God's faithfulness. The thesis is that God's word has not failed—rather, "Israel" is defined not by ethnic descent alone but by divine election. THEOLOGICAL: Paul distinguishes between ethnic Israel and the true Israel of promise, a distinction rooted in the patriarchal narratives themselves.

verses.flow: nor because they are seed of Abraham are all children, but, "In Isaac shall your seed be called."
That is, it is not the children of the flesh who are children of God, but the children of the promise are reckoned as seed.
For the word of promise is this: "According to this time I will come, and there shall be to Sarah a son."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And not only so, but also Rebecca, having conceived by one, Isaac our father—

words.m: verb aorist passive participle genitive plural masculine
verb aorist active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: for the children not yet having been born, nor having done anything good or evil, that the purpose of God according to election might stand,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The purpose clause (hina) makes God's elective purpose the ground of the declaration, explicitly prior to and independent of any human works or merit. The phrase kat' eklogēn prothesis ("the purpose according to election," G1589, G4286) is central to Paul's doctrine of divine sovereignty. TYPOLOGY: Jacob and Esau represent not merely individuals but corporate destinies—two peoples, two lines, chosen and set apart before birth (Gen 25:23).

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: not of works but of the One who calls—it was said to her, "The greater shall serve the lesser,"
just as it has been written, "Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated."
What then shall we say? Is there unrighteousness with God? May it never be!
For to Moses He says, "I will have mercy on whom I have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion."
verses.note: GREEK: Eleēsō (G1653, "I will have mercy") and oiktirēsō (G3627, "I will have compassion") are both future indicatives quoting Exodus 33:19 from the LXX. The doubled construction emphasizes the absolute freedom of God's mercy. THEOLOGICAL: God's mercy is grounded in His own will, not in any human claim or merit—this is the divine prerogative revealed to Moses after the golden calf incident.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: So then, it is not of the one willing, nor of the one running, but of God who shows mercy.
For the Scripture says to Pharaoh, "For this very purpose I raised you up, that I might demonstrate in you My power, and that My name might be proclaimed in all the earth."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Pharaoh serves as the paradigmatic example of a vessel of wrath. God "raised up" (exēgeira, G1825) Pharaoh—not merely to the throne but onto the stage of redemptive history—so that divine power would be displayed and God's name proclaimed universally (Ex 9:16). HISTORICAL: In Second Temple Jewish thought, the Exodus narrative was the supreme illustration of God's sovereign governance over pagan rulers.

verses.flow: So then, whom He wills He shows mercy, and whom He wills He hardens.
You will say to me then, "Why does He still find fault? For who has resisted His will?"

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: On the contrary, O man, who are you, the one answering back to God? Shall the thing formed say to the one who formed it, "Why did you make me thus?"
verses.note: GREEK: Antapokrinomenos (G470) means "answering back against," a strong term for presumptuous disputation with God. Plasma (G4110, "the thing formed") echoes Isaiah 29:16 and 45:9 LXX, where the clay-potter imagery rebukes human presumption against the Creator. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The potter-clay metaphor establishes an absolute ontological distinction between Creator and creature that precludes any creaturely claim against divine decisions.

verses.flow: Or does not the potter have authority over the clay, from the same lump to make one vessel unto honor, and another unto dishonor?

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect passive participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: And what if God, willing to demonstrate His wrath and to make known His power, bore with much longsuffering vessels of wrath having been fitted for destruction,
verses.note: GREEK: Katērtismena (G2675, perfect passive participle, "having been fitted") is debated—the passive may indicate self-preparation or divine preparation for destruction. The term apōleia (G684) denotes utter ruin or perdition. THEOLOGICAL: God's longsuffering (makrothumia, G3115) toward vessels of wrath serves His larger redemptive purpose, displaying both justice and patience simultaneously.

verses.flow: and that He might make known the riches of His glory upon vessels of mercy, which He prepared beforehand unto glory,
whom He also called—us—not only from among Jews, but also from among Gentiles?

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular feminine
verb perfect passive participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: As also in Hosea He says, "I will call the not-My-people, My people, and the not-beloved, beloved."

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: "And it shall be, in the place where it was said to them, 'You are not My people,' there they shall be called sons of the living God."
And Isaiah cries out concerning Israel, "Though the number of the sons of Israel be as the sand of the sea, the remnant shall be saved;

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: for the Lord will execute His word upon the earth, finishing and cutting it short."
And just as Isaiah foretold, "Unless the Lord of Sabaoth had left us a seed, we would have become as Sodom, and we would have been made like Gomorrah."
verses.note: GREEK: Sabaōth (G4519) is a transliteration of Hebrew tseva'ot ("of hosts/armies"), preserved untranslated from Isaiah 1:9 LXX. DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Lord of Sabaoth" designates God as commander of the heavenly armies—the divine council host—underscoring His sovereign authority over both heavenly and earthly realms. The remnant theology shows God's faithfulness preserved even through judgment.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: What then shall we say? That Gentiles, the ones not pursuing righteousness, attained righteousness, and that the righteousness from faith;

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: but Israel, pursuing a law of righteousness, did not attain to that law.
Why? Because it was not from faith, but as from works. They stumbled against the stone of stumbling,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: just as it has been written, "Behold, I lay in Zion a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense, and the one believing upon Him shall not be put to shame."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul conflates Isaiah 28:16 and Isaiah 8:14, identifying Christ as both the precious cornerstone and the stone of stumbling. This dual function of the same stone—salvation for believers, offense for unbelievers—encapsulates the paradox of the gospel. THEOLOGICAL: "The one believing upon Him shall not be put to shame" (ou kataischynthēsetai) reframes the Isaiah promise christologically, making faith in Christ the decisive criterion (cf. Rom 10:11; 1 Pet 2:6-8).

verses.flow: Brothers, the desire of my heart and my prayer to God on behalf of them is for salvation.
For I bear witness to them that they have a zeal for God, but not according to knowledge.

verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For being ignorant of the righteousness of God, and seeking to establish their own, they did not submit themselves to the righteousness of God.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: For Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes.
verses.note: GREEK: "Telos" (τέλος, Strong's G5056) can mean "end," "goal," "completion," or "fulfillment." Paul's usage here carries both senses: Christ is the fulfillment toward which the law aimed and the termination of the law as a means of establishing righteousness. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is pivotal to Paul's argument — the Torah was never the ultimate means of righteousness but pointed forward to Christ, in whom the law's righteous requirement finds its completion (Rom 8:4).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For Moses writes that the man who has done the righteousness that is from the law shall live by it.
But the righteousness that is from faith speaks thus: "Do not say in your heart, 'Who shall ascend into heaven?'" — that is, to bring Christ down —
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul adapts Deuteronomy 30:12-14 (LXX), where Moses spoke of the accessibility of the commandment. Paul reinterprets the passage christologically: the "word" that is near is now the word of faith concerning Christ. The ascent to heaven and descent to the abyss correspond to Christ's incarnation and resurrection, showing that God has already accomplished what the law could not. STRUCTURE: The rhetorical questions form a chiastic pattern with verse 7, centering on Christ's saving work.

verses.flow: "Or, 'Who shall descend into the abyss?'" — that is, to bring Christ up from the dead.
But what does it say? "The word is near you, in your mouth and in your heart" — that is, the word of faith which we proclaim.
That if you confess with your mouth Jesus as Lord, and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you shall be saved.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse contains the foundational pattern of Christian confession. "Confess" (ὁμολογέω, homologeō, G3670) means to speak the same thing, to publicly declare agreement. Confessing "Jesus as Lord" (κύριον Ἰησοῦν) directly applies the title Kyrios — used of YHWH in the LXX — to Jesus, constituting a divine claim. The pairing of mouth-confession and heart-belief echoes the Deuteronomy 30:14 citation in verse 8.

verses.flow: For with the heart one believes unto righteousness, and with the mouth one confesses unto salvation.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the Scripture says, "Everyone who believes upon him shall not be put to shame."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Isaiah 28:16 (LXX), adding "everyone" (πᾶς) to emphasize the universal scope of the promise. In its original context, the stone is the foundation stone in Zion; Paul identifies this as Christ, the cornerstone upon whom faith rests (cf. Rom 9:33; 1 Pet 2:6).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: For there is no distinction between Jew and Greek, for the same Lord of all is rich toward all who call upon him.
For "everyone who calls upon the name of the Lord shall be saved."
verses.note: GREEK: Paul quotes Joel 2:32 (LXX Joel 3:5). The phrase "the name of the Lord" (τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου) in Joel refers to YHWH. Paul applies this to Jesus, given the immediate context of verse 9 where Jesus is confessed as Lord. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This transfer of the divine Name to Jesus reflects the early Christian conviction that Jesus shares in the divine identity and exercises the eschatological authority of YHWH himself.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: How then shall they call upon him in whom they have not believed? And how shall they believe in him of whom they have not heard? And how shall they hear apart from one proclaiming?

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: And how shall they proclaim unless they are sent? As it has been written: "How beautiful are the feet of those who proclaim good things."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Isaiah 52:7 (LXX), which originally proclaimed the return of YHWH to Zion and the good news of Israel's deliverance from exile. Paul applies this to the apostolic mission of proclaiming the gospel of Christ, identifying the new exodus salvation with the preaching of Christ's death and resurrection.

verses.flow: But not all obeyed the gospel. For Isaiah says, "Lord, who has believed our report?"
So then, faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: "Rhēmatos" (ῥήματος, G4487) denotes a spoken utterance or proclamation, distinct from "logos." Some manuscripts read "word of God" (θεοῦ) instead of "word of Christ" (Χριστοῦ); the harder reading "of Christ" is likely original. THEOLOGICAL: Faith is not self-generated but arises from the proclaimed word — the kerygma concerning Christ. This establishes the necessity of the preaching chain described in verses 14-15.

verses.flow: But I say, did they not hear? Indeed: "Into all the earth their sound has gone out, and unto the ends of the inhabited world their words."
But I say, did Israel not know? First, Moses says, "I will provoke you to jealousy by that which is not a nation; by a foolish nation I will anger you."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Deuteronomy 32:21 from the Song of Moses, where God warns that he will provoke Israel to jealousy through a "non-nation" and a "foolish nation." Paul reads this as prophetic of God's inclusion of the Gentiles, which serves the purpose of provoking Israel to return. HISTORICAL: The "Song of Moses" (Deut 32) was widely recognized in Second Temple Judaism as a prophetic text concerning Israel's future judgment and restoration.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine
verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: And Isaiah is very bold and says, "I was found by those who did not seek me; I became manifest to those who did not ask for me."

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: But concerning Israel he says, "All the day long I stretched out my hands toward a disobedient and contrary people."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul quotes Isaiah 65:2 (LXX) as God's own lament over Israel. The image of outstretched hands signifies persistent divine invitation and patience. The terms "disobedient" (ἀπειθοῦντα, G544) and "contrary" (ἀντιλέγοντα, G483, literally "speaking against") characterize not ignorance but active resistance. Yet the posture of outstretched hands implies that God's offer remains open — setting up the argument of Romans 11 that Israel's rejection is neither total nor final.

verses.flow: I say then, has God cast away His people? May it never be! For I also am an Israelite, of the seed of Abraham, of the tribe of Benjamin.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul's rhetorical question (μὴ ἀπώσατο, mē apōsato) expects a negative answer—God has emphatically not cast away His covenant people. Paul offers himself as living proof, citing his Israelite lineage through Benjamin, the tribe that remained loyal to Judah after the kingdom split (1 Kgs 12:21). GREEK: ἀπώσατο (apōsato, G683) means "to thrust away, reject utterly," echoing the LXX of Ps 94:14 (93:14 LXX) and 1 Sam 12:22.

verses.flow: God has not cast away His people whom He foreknew. Or do you not know what the Scripture says in Elijah, how he pleads with God against Israel?
verses.note: GREEK: προέγνω (proegnō, G4267) means "foreknew"—not mere foresight but relational, covenantal knowing beforehand, establishing an intimate bond prior to election. This echoes the Hebrew yada' (intimate knowing) applied to God's covenant relationship (Amos 3:2). THEOLOGICAL: God's foreknowledge grounds His faithfulness; the people He set His love upon He will not abandon.

verses.flow: "Lord, they have killed Your prophets, they have torn down Your altars, and I alone am left, and they seek my life."
But what says the divine answer to him? "I have kept for Myself seven thousand men who have not bowed the knee to Baal."
verses.note: GREEK: χρηματισμός (chrēmatismos, G5538) is a NT hapax legomenon, occurring only here. It denotes a divine oracle or formal response from God, carrying the weight of an official decree. TYPOLOGY: The 7,000 faithful in Elijah's day (1 Kgs 19:18) prefigure the believing remnant in Paul's day—God always preserves a faithful seed within Israel.

verses.flow: So then, also in the present time a remnant according to the election of grace has come to be.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: λεῖμμα (leimma, G3005) means "remnant" and occurs only here in the NT. Paul connects the remnant theology of the prophets (Isa 10:22; 1:9) to the present age. The remnant exists κατ' ἐκλογὴν χάριτος (kat' eklogēn charitos)—"according to election of grace"—making clear that the remnant is constituted by divine choice, not human merit.

verses.flow: And if by grace, it is no longer of works; otherwise grace no longer becomes grace.
What then? What Israel seeks after, this it did not obtain; but the election obtained it, and the rest were hardened,
just as it is written: "God gave them a spirit of stupor, eyes not to see, and ears not to hear, until this very day."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul combines Deut 29:4 and Isa 29:10 to show that judicial hardening is a recurring pattern in Israel's history. The "spirit of stupor" (πνεῦμα κατανύξεως, pneuma katanyxeōs) denotes a divine stupefaction—God giving over to spiritual insensibility those who persistently reject Him. GREEK: κατάνυξις (katanyxis, G2659) is a NT hapax, meaning "deep sleep, numbness, piercing torpor."

verses.flow: And David says: "Let their table become a snare and a trap, and a stumbling block and a recompense to them;
let their eyes be darkened so as not to see, and their back bow down always."
I say then, did they stumble in order that they might fall? May it never be! But by their trespass, salvation has come to the Gentiles, so as to provoke them to jealousy.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul's second μὴ γένοιτο (mē genoito, "may it never be!") in this chapter guards against the conclusion that Israel's stumbling was purposeless. Their παράπτωμα (paraptōma, "trespass/fall") became the providential means by which salvation reached the Gentiles. TYPOLOGY: This echoes Joseph's statement to his brothers: "You meant it for evil, but God meant it for good" (Gen 50:20)—Israel's rejection of Messiah opened the door for the nations.

words.g: which is put in to fill up

verses.flow: Now if their trespass is riches for the world, and their diminishing is riches for the Gentiles, how much more their fullness!
But to you I speak, the Gentiles—inasmuch as I am an apostle of the Gentiles, I glorify my ministry—
if somehow I might provoke to jealousy my flesh and save some of them.
For if their casting away is the reconciliation of the world, what will their acceptance be, if not life from the dead?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: ἀποβολή (apobolē, G580) means "casting away/rejection." If Israel's temporary rejection resulted in cosmic reconciliation (καταλλαγὴ κόσμου), then their πρόσλημψις (proslēmpsis, G4356, "acceptance/receiving," another near-hapax) will be nothing less than "life from the dead"—language pointing either to eschatological resurrection or a massive spiritual awakening of global proportions.

verses.flow: And if the firstfruit is holy, so also the lump; and if the root is holy, so also the branches.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But if some of the branches were broken off, and you, being a wild olive tree, were grafted in among them, and became a fellow partaker of the root and of the fatness of the olive tree,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The olive tree metaphor draws on Jer 11:16 where God calls Israel "a green olive tree, beautiful in fruit and form." Wild olive branches (Gentile believers) grafted into the cultivated tree share in the covenantal promises made to the patriarchs. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The image of engrafting into the root implies participation in the Abrahamic covenant blessings, not replacement of Israel—Gentiles become co-heirs, not substitutes (Eph 3:6).

verses.flow: do not boast against the branches. But if you boast, it is not you who bears the root, but the root bears you.
You will say then, "Branches were broken off so that I might be grafted in."
Well said. By unbelief they were broken off, and you stand by faith. Do not be high-minded, but fear.
For if God did not spare the natural branches, neither will He spare you.

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Behold then the kindness and severity of God: upon those who fell, severity; but upon you, the kindness of God, if you continue in His kindness; otherwise you also will be cut off.
And they also, if they do not continue in unbelief, will be grafted in, for God is able to graft them in again.

For if you were cut off from the olive tree which is wild by nature, and were grafted contrary to nature into a cultivated olive tree, how much more will these, the natural branches, be grafted into their own olive tree?

words.g: which is put in to fill up

verses.flow: For I do not want you to be ignorant, brothers, of this mystery, lest you be wise in yourselves: that a hardening in part has come upon Israel, until the fullness of the Gentiles has come in.
verses.note: GREEK: μυστήριον (mystērion, G3466) denotes not something mysterious but a divine secret now revealed. THEOLOGICAL: The revealed mystery has three components: (1) Israel's hardening is partial (ἀπὸ μέρους), (2) it is temporary—lasting until the πλήρωμα τῶν ἐθνῶν ("fullness of the Gentiles") enters, and (3) it results in all Israel being saved (v.26). This "fullness of the Gentiles" likely echoes Gen 48:19 (LXX: πλῆθος ἐθνῶν) and Jesus' "times of the Gentiles" (Luke 21:24).

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And so all Israel shall be saved, just as it is written: "The Deliverer shall come out of Zion, and He shall turn away ungodliness from Jacob.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Isa 59:20-21 and 27:9 from the LXX. "The Deliverer" (ὁ ῥυόμενος, ho rhyomenos) coming "out of Zion" identifies the Messiah—Christ—as the One who turns away ungodliness from Jacob. In the Hebrew of Isa 59:20 the Redeemer comes "to Zion," but the LXX reads "out of Zion," emphasizing that salvation originates from God's appointed place and person. THEOLOGICAL: "All Israel" (πᾶς Ἰσραήλ) most likely refers to ethnic Israel as a corporate whole in the eschatological future, not merely the elect remnant.

verses.flow: And this is My covenant with them, when I take away their sins."
According to the gospel, they are enemies on account of you; but according to the election, they are beloved on account of the fathers.
For the gifts and the calling of God are irrevocable.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀμεταμέλητα (ametamelēta, G278) means "not to be repented of, irrevocable." God does not rescind His χαρίσματα (charismata, "grace-gifts") or His κλῆσις (klēsis, "calling"). THEOLOGICAL: This is a foundational statement on divine faithfulness—the covenantal promises to Israel through Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob remain operative because God's character does not change (Mal 3:6; Num 23:19).

verses.flow: For just as you once were disobedient to God, but now have been shown mercy by their disobedience,
so also these now have been disobedient to your mercy, in order that they also may now be shown mercy.
For God has shut up all into disobedience, in order that He might show mercy to all.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: συνέκλεισεν (synekleisen, G4788) means "shut up together, enclosed, confined." God has confined all—both Jew and Gentile—under the condition of disobedience so that mercy might be the universal ground of salvation. No one stands before God on the basis of obedience; all stand only by mercy. This summarizes the entire argument of Romans 1-11. STRUCTURE: This verse serves as the theological climax before the doxology of vv.33-36.

verses.flow: O the depth of the riches and wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable are His judgments, and untraceable His ways!
verses.note: GREEK: ἀνεξεραύνητα (anexeraunēta, G419) means "unsearchable, beyond investigation"—a NT hapax. ἀνεξιχνίαστοι (anexichniastoi, G421) means "untraceable, past finding out," used elsewhere only in Eph 3:8. THEOLOGICAL: This doxology celebrates the incomprehensibility of God's wisdom in orchestrating salvation history through both Israel's hardening and the Gentiles' inclusion, revealing a plan no human mind could have conceived.

verses.flow: "For who has known the mind of the Lord? Or who has become His counselor?"
"Or who has first given to Him, and it shall be repaid to him?"
For from Him and through Him and unto Him are all things. To Him be the glory unto the ages. Amen.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The preposition triad ἐξ αὐτοῦ (from Him—source), δι' αὐτοῦ (through Him—means), εἰς αὐτόν (unto Him—goal) establishes God as the origin, sustainer, and telos of all things. This is one of the most comprehensive statements of divine sovereignty in Scripture. STRUCTURE: This verse concludes the entire doctrinal section of Romans (chs.1-11) with a declaration that all of redemptive history—Israel's call, the Gentiles' inclusion, and the final restoration—redounds to God's eternal glory.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: I beseech you therefore, brothers, through the mercies of God, to present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, well-pleasing to God, which is your reasonable service.
verses.note: GREEK: "Parakalō" (G3870) carries the sense of earnest appeal, not mere suggestion. "Logikēn latreian" (λογικὴν λατρείαν) — "reasonable service" — is debated; "logikos" (G3050) can mean "rational," "spiritual," or "word-based." "Latreia" (G2999) is cultic/priestly service, the same term used of temple worship. THEOLOGICAL: This verse pivots the entire epistle from doctrinal exposition (chs. 1–11) to ethical exhortation (chs. 12–16). The language of sacrifice (thysia, G2378) and priestly presentation (paristēmi, G3936) transforms the believer's body into a living temple offering — replacing the dead animal sacrifices of the old covenant with a living, holy, ongoing presentation. TYPOLOGY: The "living sacrifice" inverts the Levitical pattern where the offering died; believers are called to die to self yet live as the offering (Gal 2:20).

verses.flow: And be not conformed to this age, but be transformed by the renewing of the mind, that you may prove what is the will of God, the good and well-pleasing and perfect.
verses.note: GREEK: "Syschēmatizesthe" (G4964) means to be outwardly molded to a pattern — from "schēma" (external form). "Metamorphousthe" (G3339) denotes an inward, organic transformation — the same word used of Christ's transfiguration (Matt 17:2). "Anakainōsis" (G342) — "renewing" — is a NT hapax legomenon outside of Titus 3:5, suggesting a radical, once-for-all newness. THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between conformity to "this age" (aiōn, G165) and transformation by renewed mind establishes the eschatological tension in which believers live — the present evil age versus the age inaugurated by Christ's resurrection.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle genitive singular feminine
verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: For I say, through the grace having been given to me, to everyone who is among you, not to think of himself more highly than he ought to think, but to think so as to think soberly, to each as God has apportioned a measure of faith.
verses.note: GREEK: Paul employs a remarkable wordplay on "phronein" (φρονεῖν, G5426): hyperphronein (think too highly), phronein (think), sōphronein (think soberly/rightly). This triple repetition emphasizes that right thinking about oneself is the foundation of community life. "Metron pisteos" (measure of faith) indicates God sovereignly distributes differing capacities of faith-enabled service to each believer.

verses.flow: For even as in one body we have many members, yet all the members have not the same function,
so we, the many, are one body in Christ, and individually members of one another.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "one body in Christ" ecclesiology echoes 1 Cor 12:12–27 and anticipates Eph 4:4–16. The phrase "members of one another" (allēlōn melē) is striking — believers do not merely belong to Christ but are organically joined to each other. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The unity of the body under Christ as Head reflects the cosmic ordering of all things under the one Lord, in contrast to the fractured allegiances of the nations under lesser spiritual beings (cf. Deut 32:8–9; Eph 1:22–23).

verses.flow: And having gifts differing according to the grace having been given to us, whether prophecy, let it be according to the proportion of the faith;

verses.flow: whether the one exhorting, in the exhortation; the one giving, in simplicity; the one leading, in diligence; the one showing mercy, in cheerfulness.
verses.note: GREEK: "Haplotēs" (G572), translated "simplicity," can also mean "generosity" or "sincerity" — the one who gives should do so without ulterior motive or double-mindedness. "Hilarotēs" (G2432) — "cheerfulness" — appears only here in the NT (hapax legomenon); it is the root of English "hilarity." LXX uses the cognate in Prov 22:8a (LXX): "God loves a cheerful giver" (cf. 2 Cor 9:7).

verses.flow: Let love be without hypocrisy. Abhorring the evil, cleaving to the good.
verses.note: GREEK: "Anypokritos" (G505) — "without hypocrisy" — literally "un-masked," from the theatrical term "hypokritēs" (actor). STRUCTURE: Verses 9–21 shift to a rapid series of participial and imperatival phrases — short, staccato ethical commands that form a distinct literary unit sometimes called the "ethical maxims" of Paul, resembling wisdom literature in form.

verses.flow: In brotherly love, toward one another be tenderly affectionate; in honor, esteeming one another above yourselves.

verses.flow: In hope, rejoicing; in tribulation, enduring; in prayer, persevering.

verses.flow: Contributing to the needs of the saints; pursuing hospitality.

verses.flow: Bless those who persecute you; bless, and do not curse.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This command directly echoes Jesus' teaching in the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:44; Luke 6:28). "Diōkontas" (G1377) — "those persecuting" — uses the same root as "pursuing" hospitality in v13 (diōkontes), creating an ironic wordplay: pursue hospitality, and bless those who pursue you with hostility.
