verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: Rejoice with those who rejoice; weep with those who weep.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Being of the same mind toward one another; not setting the mind on high things, but being led along with the lowly. Be not wise in your own eyes.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Repaying to no one evil for evil; giving forethought to things honorable before all men.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: If it be possible, so far as it depends on you, being at peace with all men.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Not avenging yourselves, beloved, but give place to the wrath; for it has been written, "Vengeance is Mine; I will repay," says the Lord.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Give place to the wrath" (dote topon tē orgē) — "the wrath" with the article likely refers to God's eschatological wrath, not human anger. Believers are to step aside and allow God to execute justice. The quotation is from Deut 32:35 (LXX), part of the Song of Moses — a passage deeply rooted in divine council theology where God asserts His sole right to judge and avenge His people. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Deut 32 is the key divine council text where God judges the "sons of God" (elohim) assigned over the nations and reclaims vengeance as His own prerogative (Deut 32:8–9, 35, 43).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But if your enemy hungers, feed him; if he thirsts, give him drink; for in doing this you will heap coals of fire upon his head.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Prov 25:21–22 (LXX). "Coals of fire upon his head" (anthrakas pyros sōreuseis) is debated: it may refer to an Egyptian repentance ritual where penitents carried coals on their heads as a sign of contrition, or it may signify the burning conviction that kindness produces in an enemy — thus heaping divine judgment or redemptive shame. THEOLOGICAL: The ethic of enemy-love is grounded not in passivity but in the confidence that God will set all things right (v19), freeing the believer to respond with aggressive goodness.

verses.flow: Be not overcome by evil, but overcome evil with the good.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse summarizes the entire ethical section of vv. 9–21 with a chiastic contrast: evil/overcome vs. overcome/evil/good. The imperative "nika" (G3528, conquer/overcome) frames the Christian life as spiritual warfare waged not with violence but with the good — the same victory language used of Christ in Rev 5:5 and believers in 1 John 5:4–5.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural feminine
verb present active participle nominative plural feminine
verb perfect passive participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: Let every soul be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been appointed by God.
verses.note: GREEK: "Every soul" (πᾶσα ψυχή, pasa psychē) is a Hebraism meaning "every person," using psychē (Strong's G5590) in its sense of the whole person, not merely the immaterial soul. "Governing authorities" (ἐξουσίαις ὑπερεχούσαις) uses exousia (G1849, "delegated authority/power") with the participle of hyperechō (G5242, "to hold above, surpass"), indicating authorities that stand in a position of superiority. "Appointed" (τεταγμέναι, tetagmenai) is a perfect passive participle of tassō (G5021), a military term meaning "to arrange in order," indicating that existing authorities have been set in their ordered position by God. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Paul's assertion that no authority exists except from God resonates with the divine council framework in which God delegates governance — both heavenly and earthly — under His sovereign arrangement (cf. Dan 2:21, Dan 4:17, Deut 32:8 LXX).

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: So then, the one who sets himself against the authority has resisted the ordinance of God; and those who have resisted shall receive judgment upon themselves.
verses.note: GREEK: "Sets himself against" (ἀντιτασσόμενος, antitassomenos, G498) is formed from anti ("against") + tassō ("to arrange/order"), creating a deliberate wordplay with the tetagmenai ("appointed/ordered") of v1. The one who opposes the arrangement opposes God's own ordering. "Ordinance" (διαταγῇ, diatagē, G1296) is a rare word in the NT (only here and Acts 7:53), denoting a decree or institution formally established.

verses.flow: For the rulers are not a terror to the good work, but to the evil. And do you desire not to fear the authority? Do that which is good, and you shall have praise from it.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: For it is a servant of God to you for the good. But if you do that which is evil, be afraid; for it does not bear the sword in vain. For it is a servant of God, an avenger for wrath upon the one who practices evil.
verses.note: GREEK: "Servant" (διάκονος, diakonos, G1249) designates one who serves another's purposes — here the governing authority serves God's purpose. "Sword" (μάχαιραν, machairan, G3162) refers to the short Roman sword, the symbol of the magistrate's ius gladii — the right of capital punishment. "Avenger" (ἔκδικος, ekdikos, G1558) means one who carries out justice or punishment. HISTORICAL: Roman provincial governors held the ius gladii, the authority to execute capital punishment, symbolized by the sword carried before them in public processions. Paul writes to believers living under direct Roman imperial administration in the capital itself.

verses.flow: Therefore it is necessary to be subject, not only on account of wrath, but also on account of conscience.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For on account of this you also pay taxes, for they are ministers of God, attending continually to this very thing.
Render to all their dues: to whom tax is owed, the tax; to whom custom is owed, the custom; to whom fear is owed, the fear; to whom honor is owed, the honor.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul's language of rendering what is owed echoes Jesus' own teaching: "Render to Caesar the things of Caesar, and to God the things of God" (Matt 22:21, Mark 12:17, Luke 20:25). The fourfold structure — tax, custom, fear, honor — moves from material obligation to relational reverence. GREEK: "Render" (ἀπόδοτε, apodote, G591) carries the force of giving back what is already owed, not a voluntary gift but a debt repaid.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Owe no one anything, except to love one another; for the one who loves the other has fulfilled the law.

For the commandments, "You shall not commit adultery," "You shall not murder," "You shall not steal," "You shall not covet," and if there is any other commandment, it is summed up in this word: "You shall love your neighbor as yourself."
verses.note: GREEK: "Summed up" (ἀνακεφαλαιοῦται, anakephalaioutai, G346) is a rare word meaning "to bring under one head, to recapitulate" — from ana + kephalē ("head"). All individual commandments find their recapitulation under the single heading of love. TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes the Decalogue commands (Exod 20:13-17, Deut 5:17-21) and then Lev 19:18 as their summation, following the same hermeneutical pattern Jesus used (Matt 22:37-40). The law finds its telos in the love command.

words.g: which is put in to fill up

verses.flow: Love works no evil to the neighbor. Therefore love is the fulfillment of the law.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Fulfillment" (πλήρωμα, plērōma, G4138) means "that which fills up, fullness, completion." Love is not merely one obligation among many but the full content and completion of the law's entire demand. This connects to Paul's broader argument that believers, empowered by the Spirit, fulfill the righteous requirement of the law (Rom 8:4).

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And this, knowing the time, that it is already the hour for you to be raised from sleep; for now our salvation is nearer than when we first believed.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The time" (τὸν καιρόν, ton kairon, G2540) denotes not chronological time (chronos) but the decisive, appointed season — the eschatological moment in which believers live between Christ's resurrection and His return. "Raised from sleep" (ἐξ ὕπνου ἐγερθῆναι) uses egeirō (G1453), the same verb regularly used for resurrection, creating a deliberate double resonance: moral awakening now prefigures the bodily resurrection to come. "Salvation is nearer" places the believer in an already/not-yet tension — salvation inaugurated at faith, consummated at the parousia.

verses.flow: The night has advanced, and the day has drawn near. Let us therefore cast off the works of darkness, and let us put on the armor of the light.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The night" and "the day" constitute an eschatological metaphor: the present evil age is the night that is passing, and the coming age of Christ's kingdom is the day drawing near. "Armor of the light" (τὰ ὅπλα τοῦ φωτός) uses hopla (G3696), full military armament, not merely clothing — believers are engaged in spiritual warfare in the overlap of the ages (cf. Eph 6:11-17, 1 Thess 5:8, 2 Cor 6:7). STRUCTURE: Verses 11-14 form a distinct eschatological exhortation unit, shifting from civic duties (vv. 1-7) and the love command (vv. 8-10) to urgent end-times vigilance.

verses.flow: Let us walk becomingly, as in the day — not in reveling and drunkenness, not in sexual immorality and sensuality, not in strife and jealousy.
But put on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make no provision for the flesh, unto its desires.
verses.note: GREEK: "Put on" (ἐνδύσασθε, endysasthe, G1746) is an aorist middle imperative — a decisive, once-for-all act of clothing oneself with Christ as one would put on armor or a garment. This is not metaphorical in a weak sense; it echoes baptismal identity (Gal 3:27, "as many as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ"). "Provision" (πρόνοιαν, pronoian, G4307) means forethought or planning — believers are not merely to avoid sin but to refuse to premeditate the fulfillment of fleshly desires. HISTORICAL: Augustine famously records in Confessions (VIII.12.29) that this verse was the catalyst for his conversion, hearing "tolle lege" ("take up, read") and opening to this very passage.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the one being weak in the faith, receive, not unto disputes of reasonings.
verses.note: GREEK: "Asthenountas" (ἀσθενοῦντα, Strong's G770) means "being weak" or "being without strength" — here applied to faith (τῇ πίστει, dative of reference). "Proslambanesthe" (G4355) is a middle imperative meaning "receive to yourselves," implying warm, personal welcome. "Diakriseis dialogismōn" denotes "passing judgment on disputable opinions" — not correction of doctrine but quarreling over matters of conscience.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: One indeed believes to eat all things, but the one being weak eats vegetables.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Let not the one eating despise the one not eating, and let not the one not eating judge the one eating, for God has received him.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Who are you, the one judging another's household servant? To his own Lord he stands or falls. And he shall be made to stand, for the Lord is able to make him stand.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul establishes that each believer is a household servant (oiketēs, G3610) belonging to his own Lord (κυρίῳ). The right to judge a servant belongs exclusively to the master. The verb "stēkei" (G4739) denotes standing firm in one's position. Paul's assurance that "the Lord is able to make him stand" affirms divine sustaining power over the believer's perseverance (Rom 8:31-39).

verses.flow: One indeed judges one day above another day, and another judges every day alike. Let each one be fully assured in his own mind.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one who regards the day, regards it to the Lord. And the one who eats, eats to the Lord, for he gives thanks to God. And the one who does not eat, to the Lord he does not eat, and gives thanks to God.
For none of us lives to himself, and none dies to himself.
For if indeed we live, to the Lord we live; and if indeed we die, to the Lord we die. Whether then we live or whether we die, we are the Lord's.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: For unto this Christ both died and lived again, that He might exercise lordship over both the dead and the living.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is the theological center of the passage. Christ's death and resurrection establish His lordship (kyrieusē, G2961 — "exercise sovereign dominion") over both realms: the dead and the living. This universal dominion is the ground upon which no believer may usurp Christ's prerogative of judgment. TYPOLOGY: The pattern echoes Philippians 2:9-11 — Christ's exaltation through death grants Him the Name above every name and universal lordship.

verses.flow: But you, why do you judge your brother? Or you also, why do you despise your brother? For we shall all stand before the judgment seat of God.
For it is written: "As I live, says the Lord, that to Me every knee shall bow, and every tongue shall confess to God."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Isaiah 45:23 (LXX), where YHWH speaks of universal submission to Himself. By applying this text in a context where "Lord" refers to Christ (vv. 8-9), Paul identifies Jesus with the God of Israel who receives the worship of every knee. GREEK: "Exomologēsetai" (G1843) means "shall openly confess/acknowledge" — a public declaration of God's righteous authority. This passage is also cited in Philippians 2:10-11 with explicit reference to Christ.

verses.flow: So then each of us shall give an account concerning himself to God.
Therefore let us no longer judge one another, but rather determine this: not to place a stumbling block or a cause of falling before the brother.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: I know and have been persuaded in the Lord Jesus that nothing is common through itself; except to the one reckoning anything to be common, to that one it is common.
verses.note: GREEK: "Koinon" (κοινόν, G2839) means "common" or "profane" in the Levitical sense — ceremonially unclean, not shared for common use. Paul's conviction "in the Lord Jesus" that nothing is inherently unclean marks a decisive theological shift from Torah dietary regulations (cf. Mark 7:19, Acts 10:15). HISTORICAL: This language directly addresses tensions between Jewish and Gentile believers in Rome regarding food purity laws from Leviticus 11 and Second Temple halakhic tradition.

verses.flow: For if on account of food your brother is grieved, you are no longer walking according to love. Do not by your food destroy that one for whom Christ died.
Therefore let not your good be blasphemed.
For the kingdom of God is not eating and drinking, but righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul defines the kingdom of God (basileía tou theou) not by external observances but by three spiritual realities: righteousness (dikaiosynē, G1343), peace (eirēnē, G1515), and joy (chara, G5479) — all located "in the Holy Spirit" (en pneumati hagiō). This is one of the clearest Pauline definitions of God's kingdom as a present spiritual reality, not merely eschatological. The triad reflects the fruit of the Spirit (Gal 5:22).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the one serving Christ in this is well-pleasing to God and approved among men.
So then, let us pursue the things of peace and the things of building up one another.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: Do not for the sake of food tear down the work of God. All things indeed are clean, but it is evil for the man who eats through a stumbling block.
verses.note: GREEK: "Katalye" (G2647) means "to tear down, demolish" — the opposite of "oikodomē" (building up) in v. 19. "Kathara" (G2513) — "all things are clean" — echoes Jesus' declaration in Mark 7:19. THEOLOGICAL: Paul holds two truths simultaneously: all foods are intrinsically clean, yet eating becomes morally evil (kakon, G2556) when it causes another to stumble through a "proskommatos" (stumbling block, G4348). Liberty is real but must be governed by love.

verses.flow: It is good not to eat flesh, nor to drink wine, nor anything in which your brother stumbles or is caused to fall or is made weak.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The faith which you have, have it according to yourself before God. Blessed is the one who does not condemn himself in what he approves.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But the one doubting, if he eats, has been condemned, because it is not from faith; and everything which is not from faith is sin.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Diakrinomenos" (G1252) here means "the one who doubts/wavers within himself" — acting against the testimony of one's own conscience. The principle "everything which is not from faith is sin" (pan de ho ouk ek pisteōs hamartia estin) is among the most far-reaching ethical statements in the New Testament. Faith here functions as the subjective conviction of conscience aligned with trust in God; action taken against that conviction constitutes sin regardless of the act's intrinsic morality.

verses.flow: Now we who are strong ought to bear the weaknesses of those not strong, and not to please ourselves.
Let each one of us please the neighbor unto the good, toward building up.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: For even the Christ did not please himself, but as it has been written, "The reproaches of those reproaching you fell upon me."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Psalm 69:9 (LXX 68:10), a messianic psalm of the righteous sufferer. Paul applies the reproaches borne by the psalmist directly to Christ, establishing the pattern that Christ bore suffering not for his own sake but vicariously on behalf of others. This psalm is among the most frequently cited OT passages in the NT (John 2:17; John 15:25; Acts 1:20).

verses.flow: For whatever things were written beforehand were written for our instruction, that through the endurance and through the encouragement of the Scriptures we might have hope.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul articulates a hermeneutical principle: all prior Scripture (τὰ προεγράφη, "things written beforehand") has a didactic purpose for the New Covenant community. The twin virtues of ὑπομονή (hypomonē, "patient endurance," Strong's G5281) and παράκλησις (paraklēsis, "encouragement/comfort," G3874) are mediated through the Scriptures themselves, producing ἐλπίς (elpis, "hope," G1680).

verses.flow: Now may the God of endurance and of encouragement grant you to think the same thing among one another according to Christ Jesus,
that with one accord, with one mouth, you may glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ.
Therefore receive one another, just as also the Christ received you, unto the glory of God.
For I say that Christ has become a servant of the circumcision on behalf of the truth of God, in order to confirm the promises made to the fathers,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Christ as διάκονον περιτομῆς ("servant of the circumcision") affirms his historical mission to Israel first, fulfilling God's covenant faithfulness (ἀλήθεια θεοῦ). The purpose clause εἰς τὸ βεβαιῶσαι ("in order to confirm") shows that Christ's ministry validated the patriarchal promises (Gen 12:1-3; 2 Sam 7:12-16), which themselves always included blessing to the nations.

verses.flow: and that the nations might glorify God on behalf of mercy, as it has been written, "Because of this I will confess you among the nations, and to your name I will sing praise."
And again he says, "Rejoice, O nations, with his people."
And again, "Praise the Lord, all the nations, and let all the peoples praise him."

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And again Isaiah says, "There shall be the root of Jesse, and the one rising up to rule the nations; upon him shall the nations hope."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Isaiah 11:10 (LXX), a key messianic prophecy. The "root of Jesse" (ῥίζα τοῦ Ἰεσσαί) identifies the Messiah as both descendant and source of the Davidic line — he rises (ἀνιστάμενος) to rule (ἄρχειν) the nations. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The verb ἄρχειν ("to rule") recalls the divine allotment of nations (Deut 32:8-9 LXX), now unified under the Messiah's singular reign, fulfilling the Abrahamic promise that all nations would find hope in him.

verses.flow: Now may the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace in believing, unto your abounding in hope, in the power of the Holy Spirit.
verses.note: GREEK: This is a prayer-wish (optative mood implied). "God of hope" (ὁ θεὸς τῆς ἐλπίδος) is a unique Pauline title for God, forming an inclusio with "God of endurance" (v.5) and "God of peace" (v.33). The phrase ἐν δυνάμει πνεύματος ἁγίου ("in the power of the Holy Spirit") identifies the Spirit as the active agent producing superabounding hope in believers.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And I myself am persuaded concerning you, my brothers, that you yourselves also are full of goodness, having been filled with all knowledge, being able also to admonish one another.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: But I have written to you the more boldly, in part, as reminding you, on account of the grace given to me by God,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine
verb perfect passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: for me to be a minister of Christ Jesus unto the nations, serving as a priest the gospel of God, that the offering of the nations might become acceptable, having been sanctified by the Holy Spirit.
verses.note: GREEK: The term λειτουργός (leitourgos, G3011) denotes a public servant or cultic minister. The participle ἱερουργοῦντα (hierourgeo, G2418) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "to serve as a priest" or "to perform sacred rites." Paul employs sacrificial-priestly language: the Gentiles themselves become the προσφορά ("offering") presented to God, sanctified by the Holy Spirit — a radical redefinition of temple worship.

verses.flow: I have therefore the boasting in Christ Jesus in the things pertaining to God.
For I will not dare to speak of anything which Christ did not work through me unto the obedience of the nations, by word and deed,
in the power of signs and wonders, in the power of the Spirit, so that from Jerusalem and in a circuit as far as Illyricum I have fulfilled the gospel of the Christ.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And thus making it my ambition to preach the gospel, not where Christ was named, lest I build upon another's foundation,
but as it has been written, "Those to whom it was not announced concerning him shall see, and those who have not heard shall understand."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Isaiah 52:15 (LXX), part of the fourth Servant Song (Isa 52:13-53:12). Paul applies the Suffering Servant's mission to his own apostolic ministry among unreached nations. Those who had received no announcement (οὐκ ἀνηγγέλη) concerning the Servant-Messiah would see and understand — a programmatic text for Gentile evangelism.

verses.flow: Therefore also I was hindered many times from coming to you.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But now, no longer having place in these regions, and having a longing to come to you for many years,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: whenever I journey unto Spain — for I hope while passing through to see you and by you to be sent forward there, if first I may be filled with your company in part —

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: but now I am journeying unto Jerusalem, ministering to the saints.
For Macedonia and Achaia were pleased to make a certain contribution for the poor among the saints in Jerusalem.
For they were pleased, and they are their debtors; for if the nations shared in their spiritual things, they ought also in the fleshly things to minister to them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul establishes the theological logic of the Gentile collection for Jerusalem: the spiritual-material reciprocity between Jewish and Gentile believers. The Gentiles are ὀφειλέται ("debtors") because they shared (ἐκοινώνησαν) in Israel's πνευματικά ("spiritual things"); therefore they are obligated (ὀφείλουσιν) to minister in σαρκικά ("fleshly/material things"). This reflects the one-people-of-God theology of Romans 9-11.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore, having completed this and having sealed to them this fruit, I will go away through you unto Spain.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

words.g: which is put in to fill up

verses.flow: And I know that in coming to you, I shall come in the fullness of the blessing of Christ.
Now I exhort you, brothers, through our Lord Jesus Christ and through the love of the Spirit, to strive together with me in prayers on my behalf unto God,
verses.note: GREEK: The verb συναγωνίσασθαι (synagōnizomai, G4865) means "to contend/strive together with," drawn from athletic competition imagery. It appears only here in the NT. Paul calls for intercessory prayer as a form of spiritual combat. The threefold invocation — "through our Lord Jesus Christ," "through the love of the Spirit," and "unto God" — is implicitly trinitarian.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: that I may be delivered from those who are disobedient in Judea, and that my ministry unto Jerusalem may become acceptable to the saints,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: that in joy, having come to you through the will of God, I may be refreshed together with you.
Now the God of peace be with you all. Amen.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: This benediction closes the body of the letter with the title "God of peace" (ὁ θεὸς τῆς εἰρήνης), forming a triad of divine titles across the chapter: God of endurance and encouragement (v.5), God of hope (v.13), and God of peace (v.33). The ἀμήν seals the prayer and marks the transition to the personal greetings of chapter 16.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: And I commend to you Phoebe our sister, being also a deacon of the assembly that is in Cenchreae,
verses.note: GREEK: "Commend" (συνίστημι, synistēmi, Strong's G4921) means to present or introduce with approval. "Deacon" (διάκονος, diakonos, G1249) here refers to one who serves in an official capacity in the assembly. Phoebe held a recognized role of service in the congregation at Cenchreae, the eastern port of Corinth. HISTORICAL: Cenchreae was a harbor town east of Corinth on the Saronic Gulf, serving as a commercial gateway to the Aegean. Paul likely entrusted this very letter to Phoebe for delivery to Rome.

verses.flow: that you may receive her in the Lord in a manner worthy of the holy ones, and that you may stand by her in whatever matter she may have need of you; for she herself also has been a patroness of many, and of myself as well.
verses.note: GREEK: "Patroness" (προστάτις, prostatis, G4368) is a hapax legomenon in the NT. It denotes a woman who is a protector, benefactor, or patron — a term of high social standing in the Greco-Roman world, indicating Phoebe used her resources and influence to support Paul and the broader community.

verses.flow: Greet Prisca and Aquila, my fellow workers in Christ Jesus,
who for the sake of my life laid down their own necks, to whom not I alone give thanks, but also all the assemblies of the nations,
and greet the assembly at their house. Greet Epaenetus my beloved, who is the firstfruit of Asia unto Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: "Firstfruit" (ἀπαρχή, aparchē, G536) denotes the first portion of a harvest offered to God. TYPOLOGY: The term echoes the OT firstfruits offering (Lev 23:10) and implies Epaenetus was the first convert in the Roman province of Asia, a consecrated beginning of the greater harvest to come (cf. 1 Cor 16:15).

verses.flow: Greet Mary, who labored much for you.
Greet Andronicus and Junia, my kinsmen and my fellow prisoners, who are notable among the apostles, who also were in Christ before me.
verses.note: GREEK: "Notable among the apostles" (ἐπίσημοι ἐν τοῖς ἀποστόλοις) — ἐπίσημος (episēmos, G1978) means "distinguished" or "marked out." This indicates Andronicus and Junia were well known and esteemed among the apostolic circle. HISTORICAL: "Junia" (Ἰουνίαν) is a feminine Latin name; early church fathers such as Chrysostom recognized her as a woman. Their being "in Christ before me" places their conversion extremely early, likely in the Jerusalem community.

verses.flow: Greet Ampliatus, my beloved in the Lord.
Greet Urbanus, our fellow worker in Christ, and Stachys my beloved.
Greet Apelles, the approved one in Christ. Greet those of the household of Aristobulus.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Greet Herodion, my kinsman. Greet those of the household of Narcissus who are in the Lord.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural feminine

verses.flow: Greet Tryphaena and Tryphosa, who labor in the Lord. Greet Persis the beloved, who labored much in the Lord.
Greet Rufus, the chosen one in the Lord, and his mother and mine.
Greet Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermes, Patrobas, Hermas, and the brothers with them.
Greet Philologus and Julia, Nereus and his sister, and Olympas, and all the holy ones with them.
Greet one another with a holy kiss. All the assemblies of Christ greet you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "holy kiss" (φίλημα ἅγιον) was a liturgical greeting expressing the unity and love of the covenant community (cf. 1 Cor 16:20; 2 Cor 13:12; 1 Thess 5:26). That "all the assemblies of Christ" send greetings underscores the universal solidarity of the body of Christ across geographic boundaries.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And I urge you, brothers, to watch out for those who are causing the divisions and the stumbling-blocks contrary to the teaching which you learned, and turn away from them.
verses.note: GREEK: "Divisions" (διχοστασίας, dichostasias, G1370) refers to dissensions or standing apart. "Stumbling-blocks" (σκάνδαλα, skandala, G4625) denotes traps or occasions of falling. THEOLOGICAL: Paul shifts sharply from warm greetings to urgent warning, a rhetorical move indicating the gravity of false teaching. The standard of judgment is "the teaching which you learned" — the apostolic deposit of doctrine (cf. Rom 6:17).

verses.flow: For such ones do not serve our Lord Christ, but their own belly, and through smooth speech and flattery they deceive the hearts of the innocent.
verses.note: GREEK: "Smooth speech" (χρηστολογία, chrēstologia, G5542) is a hapax legomenon, a compound of chrēstos ("useful, pleasant") and logos ("word") — thus, speech that sounds beneficial but is deceptive. "Flattery" (εὐλογία, eulogia, G2129) here carries the negative sense of fair-sounding blessing used to manipulate. THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between serving "our Lord Christ" versus "their own belly" echoes Phil 3:19 and exposes the self-serving motivation behind doctrinal corruption.

verses.flow: For your obedience has reached unto all; therefore I rejoice over you. And I desire you to be wise unto that which is good, and innocent unto that which is evil.
And the God of peace shall crush Satan under your feet swiftly. The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "The God of peace shall crush Satan under your feet" is a profound allusion to the protoevangelium of Gen 3:15 (LXX), where the seed of the woman would crush the serpent's head. Paul applies this promise corporately to the church united with Christ, the true Seed. The verb syntríbō (G4937, "to crush, shatter") echoes the LXX language of divine victory. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Satan is presented as the adversarial spiritual being whose defeat is certain and imminent ("swiftly," ἐν τάχει), accomplished through the God of peace working through the community of the faithful.

verses.flow: Timothy my fellow worker greets you, and Lucius and Jason and Sosipater, my kinsmen.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: I, Tertius, the one having written this epistle, greet you in the Lord.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Tertius is the amanuensis — the scribe who physically wrote the letter at Paul's dictation. This is the only place in the Pauline corpus where the secretary identifies himself by name. His personal greeting "in the Lord" shows he was a fellow believer, not merely a hired copyist. This verse provides valuable insight into the process of apostolic letter composition in the first century.

verses.flow: Gaius, my host and host of the whole assembly, greets you. Erastus the steward of the city greets you, and Quartus the brother.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Erastus (Ἔραστος) the "steward of the city" (οἰκονόμος τῆς πόλεως) held a significant civic office in Corinth. A first-century Latin inscription found in Corinth reads "Erastus, in return for his aedileship, laid this pavement at his own expense," which many scholars identify with this individual. GREEK: Oikonomos (G3623) denotes a manager or administrator of public funds, indicating a person of considerable social standing among the early believers.

verses.flow: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with all of you. Amen.
